**Zionism**[[a]](#cite_note-1) is an [ethnocultural nationalist](/wiki/Ethnic_nationalism "Ethnic nationalism")[[1]](#cite_note-2) movement that emerged in late 19th-century [Europe](/wiki/History_of_Europe#From_revolution_to_imperialism_(1789–1914) "History of Europe") to establish and support a [Jewish homeland](/wiki/Jewish_homeland "Jewish homeland") through [colonization](/wiki/Colonization "Colonization") in the [region of Palestine](/wiki/Region_of_Palestine "Region of Palestine"),[[2]](#cite_note-3) which roughly corresponds to the [Land of Israel](/wiki/Land_of_Israel "Land of Israel") in [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism")—itself central to [Jewish history](/wiki/Jewish_history "Jewish history").[[3]](#cite_note-4) Zionists wanted to create a [Jewish state](/wiki/Jewish_state "Jewish state") in Palestine with as much land, as many [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews"), and as few [Palestinian Arabs](/wiki/Palestinian_Arabs "Palestinian Arabs") as possible.[[4]](#cite_note-ZionistLandJewsArabs1-5)[[5]](#cite_note-ZionistLandJewsArabs2-6)

Zionism initially emerged in [Central](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") and [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") as a [secular](/wiki/Secular "Secular") nationalist movement in the late 19th century, in reaction to new waves of [antisemitism](/wiki/Antisemitism "Antisemitism") and in response to the [Haskalah](/wiki/Haskalah "Haskalah"), or Jewish Enlightenment.[[6]](#cite_note-7)[[7]](#cite_note-8) By the beginning of the 20th century, the Zionist movement, now led by [Theodor Herzl](/wiki/Theodor_Herzl "Theodor Herzl"), associated this national revival with Palestine, then under [Ottoman rule](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire").[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGelvin200751-9)[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPappé200610–11-10) [The arrival of Zionist settlers](/wiki/Aliyah "Aliyah") to Palestine during this period is widely seen as the start of the [Israeli–Palestinian conflict](/wiki/Israeli%E2%80%93Palestinian_conflict "Israeli–Palestinian conflict").[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasalha201270-11)[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKarsh200912-12)[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris20081-13) The Zionist claim to Palestine was based on the notion that the Jews' historical right to the land outweighed that of the Arabs.[[13]](#cite_note-OutweighedArabsBundle-14)

In 1917, the [Balfour Declaration](/wiki/Balfour_Declaration "Balfour Declaration") established [Britain's](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") support for the movement. In 1922, the [Mandate for Palestine](/wiki/Mandate_for_Palestine "Mandate for Palestine"), governed by Britain, explicitly privileged Jewish settlers over the local population. In 1948, after the end of the British Mandate, the [State of Israel](/wiki/State_of_Israel "State of Israel") [declared its independence](/wiki/Israeli_Declaration_of_Independence "Israeli Declaration of Independence") and [war](/wiki/1948_Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_War "1948 Arab–Israeli War") broke out. During the war, Israel expanded its territory to control over 78% of former Mandatory Palestine. As a result of the [1948 Palestinian expulsion and flight](/wiki/1948_Palestinian_expulsion_and_flight "1948 Palestinian expulsion and flight"), an estimated 160,000 of 870,000 Palestinians in the territory remained, forming a [Palestinian minority in Israel](/wiki/Arab_citizens_of_Israel "Arab citizens of Israel").[[14]](#cite_note-unccp-15)

Zionist views have varied over time and are not uniform, resulting in a variety of [types of Zionism](/wiki/Types_of_Zionism "Types of Zionism").[[15]](#cite_note-16) The Zionist mainstream has historically included [Liberal](/wiki/Liberal_Zionism "Liberal Zionism"), [Labor](/wiki/Labor_Zionism "Labor Zionism"), [Revisionist](/wiki/Revisionist_Zionism "Revisionist Zionism"), and [Cultural Zionism](/wiki/Cultural_Zionism "Cultural Zionism"), while groups like [Brit Shalom](/wiki/Brit_Shalom_(political_organization) "Brit Shalom (political organization)") and [Ihud](/wiki/Ihud "Ihud") have been dissident factions within the movement.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGorny1987[[Category:Wikipedia_articles_needing_page_number_citations_from_October_2024]]<sup_class="noprint_Inline-Template_"_style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i>[[Wikipedia:Citing_sources|<span_title="This_citation_requires_a_reference_to_the_specific_page_or_range_of_pages_in_which_the_material_appears. (October_2024)">page needed</span>]]</i>]</sup>-17) [Religious Zionism](/wiki/Religious_Zionism "Religious Zionism") is a variant that combines secular nationalism and religious conservatism. Advocates of Zionism have viewed it as a national [liberation movement](/wiki/Liberation_movement "Liberation movement") for the [repatriation](/wiki/Repatriation "Repatriation") of an [indigenous people](/wiki/Indigenous_people "Indigenous people") (who were subject to [persecution](/wiki/Persecution_of_Jews "Persecution of Jews") and share a [national identity](/wiki/National_identity "National identity") through [national consciousness](/wiki/National_consciousness "National consciousness")), to the [homeland](/wiki/Homeland "Homeland") of their [ancestors](/wiki/History_of_ancient_Israel_and_Judah "History of ancient Israel and Judah").[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETroen2007873–875-18)[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAaronson1996215,_224-19)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECohen2011118,_120,_128-20) [Opponents of Zionism](/wiki/Anti-Zionism "Anti-Zionism") often characterize it as a [supremacist](/wiki/Supremacist "Supremacist"),[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESabbagh-Khoury2023Conclusion-21)[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeinart2025-22)[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamal2019193–220-23) [colonialist](/wiki/Colonialist "Colonialist"),[[23]](#cite_note-CHARCOL-24) or [racist](/wiki/Zionist_racism "Zionist racism") ideology,[[24]](#cite_note-CHARRAS-25) or as a [settler colonialist](/wiki/Zionism_as_settler_colonialism "Zionism as settler colonialism") movement.[[25]](#cite_note-26)[[26]](#cite_note-27)

## Etymology and terminology

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Theodor_Herzl.jpg/250px-Theodor_Herzl.jpg)](/wiki/File:Theodor_Herzl.jpg)

[Theodor Herzl](/wiki/Theodor_Herzl "Theodor Herzl") was the founder of the modern Zionist movement. In his 1896 pamphlet *[Der Judenstaat](/wiki/Der_Judenstaat "Der Judenstaat")*, he envisioned the founding of a future independent Jewish state during the 20th century.

The word *[Zion](/wiki/Zion "Zion")* itself derives from [Mount Zion](/wiki/Mount_Zion "Mount Zion"), which is a hill in [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") and a term used in the [Hebrew Bible](/wiki/Hebrew_Bible "Hebrew Bible").[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPixner2010320–322-28)[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeusner1991469-29) It has been used poetically as a [synecdoche](/wiki/Synecdoche "Synecdoche") to refer to the Land of Israel since the period of the [Babylonian Exile](/wiki/Babylonian_Exile "Babylonian Exile"), with particular importance in Jewish messianic belief.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarel1977194–195-30)[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavidiStillmanLandauYehuda2010-31) The term also appeared in political contexts in antiquity, including on [coinage](/wiki/First_Jewish%E2%80%93Roman_War "First Jewish–Roman War") issued during the [First Jewish Revolt](/wiki/First_Jewish_Revolt "First Jewish Revolt") against Roman rule.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodblatt2006202–203-32) The messianic conception of "Zion" was utilized by the Rabbi [Zvi Hirsch Kalischer](/wiki/Zvi_Hirsch_Kalischer "Zvi Hirsch Kalischer"), who wrote *Seeking Zion* in 1862, a [proto-Zionist](/wiki/Proto-Zionist "Proto-Zionist") text which was the first written work to demand that the Jews migrate en-masse to Palestine.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELaqueur200954-33)[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBloomberg2004161-34)

The first time the term "Zion" was associated with a mass movement was with the *[Hovevei Zion](/wiki/Hovevei_Zion "Hovevei Zion")* (lit. 'Lovers of Zion'), also known as the *Hibbat Zion* (lit. 'Love of Zion'), who came together at the 1884 [Katowice Conference](/wiki/Katowice_Conference "Katowice Conference"), inspired by [Leon Pinsker](/wiki/Leon_Pinsker "Leon Pinsker")'s pamphlet *[Auto-Emancipation](/wiki/Auto-Emancipation "Auto-Emancipation")*. The first use of the term as an [-ism](/wiki/-ism "-ism") is attributed to the Austrian [Nathan Birnbaum](/wiki/Nathan_Birnbaum "Nathan Birnbaum"), in an 1890 article in his periodical *Selbst-Emancipation*, itself named after Pinsker's pamphlet.[[34]](#cite_note-35) Birnbaum used the term "Zionism" in reference to the activities of the Lovers of Zion.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPerez202325-36)[[36]](#cite_note-37) In 1893, Birnbaum published *The National Rebirth of The Jewish People in its Own Land*, which advocated for the Jews to migrate to Palestine. In the same year, Birnbaum founded "Zion: Union of Austrian Societies for the Colonization of Palestine and Syria", to carry out his proposals.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilkinson20087-38)[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobertson2001479-39)[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPauley200054-40)

The paramount Zionist leader [Theodor Herzl](/wiki/Theodor_Herzl "Theodor Herzl") was unaware of Birnbaum's original usage of the term before popularizing the term "Zionism" himself. In Herzl's diary entries between 1895 and early 1896, he originally used the term "Zionist" to describe others, and not himself. Herzl used the term "Zionist" to describe those in the Lovers of Zion, who Herzl saw as fellow Jewish nationalists, but without a concrete plan. When Herzl published *[Der Judenstaat](/wiki/Der_Judenstaat "Der Judenstaat")* in 1896, he used the term "Zionist" in the text, often in a critical light, to describe emigration advocates like the Lovers of Zion.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPenslar202337–38-41)[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPenslar202093-42) In *[Der Judenstaat](/wiki/Der_Judenstaat "Der Judenstaat")*, Herzl called for the creation of a Jewish state, an idea which he claimed was "an ancient one" that he did not "discover".[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELaqueur200984-43) After the publication of *Der Judenstaat*, Birnbaum wrote a review of the book, attributing its success to the author proudly embracing Zionism, but also criticized the cultural proposals of Herzl. Birnbaum quickly struck up a correspondence with Herzl, and gave him a copy of *The National Rebirth of The Jewish People in its Own Land*, as a sort of Zionist education.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlson201370–75-44)

Herzl did not begin self-identifying as a "Zionist" until months after the publication of *Der Judenstaat*.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPenslar202093-42) Herzl's usage of "Zionism" was later popularized when convening the 1897 [First Zionist Congress](/wiki/First_Zionist_Congress "First Zionist Congress") and the [Zionist Organization](/wiki/Zionist_Organization "Zionist Organization") founded there.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistorical_Section_of_the_Foreign_Office192021-45)

## Beliefs

### National self-determination

See also: [Nationalism](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism"), [Rise of nationalism in Europe](/wiki/Rise_of_nationalism_in_Europe "Rise of nationalism in Europe"), and [Self-determination](/wiki/Self-determination "Self-determination")

Fundamental to Zionism is the belief that Jews constitute a nation, and have a moral and historic right and need for [self-determination](/wiki/Self-determination "Self-determination"). In contrast to the Zionist notion of nationhood the Judaic sense of being a nation was rooted in religious beliefs of unique chosenness and divine providence, rather than in ethnicity. Specifically, prayers emphasized distinctiveness from other nations where a connection to [Eretz Israel](/wiki/Eretz_Israel "Eretz Israel") and the anticipation of restoration were based on messianic beliefs and religious practices, not modern nationalist conceptions.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERabkin2006A_New_Identity-46)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShimoni199553-47)

### A Jewish home and state in Palestine

Main article: [Homeland for the Jewish people](/wiki/Homeland_for_the_Jewish_people "Homeland for the Jewish people")

Further information: [Proposals for a Jewish state](/wiki/Proposals_for_a_Jewish_state "Proposals for a Jewish state")

Early Zionism was ambiguous about the form self-determination might take. While some texts spoke of a "Jewish state", others spoke only of a "Jewish homeland" (in the Basel declaration) or "national home for the Jewish people" (the term used in the Balfour Declaration), usually under the sovereignty of the Ottoman and, later, the British Empire. According to [Shlomo Avineri](/wiki/Shlomo_Avineri "Shlomo Avineri"), it was only in the 1930s that a sovereign state rather than home became the fully articulated goal.[[47]](#cite_note-48)

Similarly, some early Zionists, including Herzl, considered [possibilities](/wiki/Sixth_Zionist_Congress "Sixth Zionist Congress") for a Jewish national home or state outside Palestine, for example in the Americas or Africa. In 1905 (after Herzl's death), a majority of the Zionist Congress voted against the proposal to settle in East Africa, and the supporters of this proposal, led by [Israel Zangwill](/wiki/Israel_Zangwill "Israel Zangwill"), split from the movement to form the [Jewish Territorial Organization](/wiki/Jewish_Territorial_Organization "Jewish Territorial Organization").[[48]](#cite_note-h552-49)[[49]](#cite_note-m783-50)

Over time, the belief that Jews have a moral and historic right and need for self-determination in [Palestine](/wiki/Palestine_(region) "Palestine (region)") became the dominant Zionist view.[[b]](#cite_note-52) Zionists believed that the Jewish claim to Palestine outweighed that of the local Arabs.[[13]](#cite_note-OutweighedArabsBundle-14) After suffering as a minority in Europe and the Middle East, establishing a Jewish state, with a Jewish majority, became a focus of the Zionist movement. Zionist organizations encouraged immigration to Palestine, and antisemitism produced a strong push factor.[[51]](#cite_note-majority-53)

### Claim to a Jewish demographic majority

See also: [Demographic history of Palestine (region)](/wiki/Demographic_history_of_Palestine_(region) "Demographic history of Palestine (region)")

The establishment of a Jewish demographic majority was an essential aspect of Zionism.[[52]](#cite_note-54) Israeli historian [Yosef Gorny](/wiki/Yosef_Gorny "Yosef Gorny") argues that this demographic change required annulling the majority status of the Arabs.[[53]](#cite_note-55) Gorny argues that the Zionist movement regarded Arab motives in Palestine as lacking both moral and historical significance.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGorny1987251-56) According to Israeli historian [Simha Flapan](/wiki/Simha_Flapan "Simha Flapan"), the view expressed in [Golda Meir](/wiki/Golda_Meir "Golda Meir")'s 1969 interview comment that "[there was no such thing as Palestinians](/wiki/There_was_no_such_thing_as_Palestinians "There was no such thing as Palestinians")" is a cornerstone of Zionist policy.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlapan197912-57) After the Holocaust, even those on the far-left of the Zionist movement, including [Martin Buber](/wiki/Martin_Buber "Martin Buber") and other members of Brit Shalom, did not see the Arabs as having an equally weighty claim to nationhood in Palestine,[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJacobs2017274-58) and sought demographic parity.[[c]](#cite_note-60) [Judah Magnes](/wiki/Judah_Magnes "Judah Magnes"), even after the Holocaust, continued to support a binational state, even one imposed by the Great Powers, but was unable to find any Arab interlocutors.[[58]](#cite_note-61) British officials supporting the Zionist effort also held similar beliefs.[[d]](#cite_note-63)[[e]](#cite_note-65)[[61]](#cite_note-66)

Unlike other forms of nationalism, the Zionist claim to Palestine was aspirational and required a mechanism by which the claim could be realized.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPenslar20231–2-67) The territorial concentration of Jews in Palestine and the subsequent goal of establishing a Jewish majority there was the main mechanism by which Zionist groups sought to realize this claim.[[63]](#cite_note-68) By the time of the [1936 Arab Revolt](/wiki/1936_Arab_Revolt "1936 Arab Revolt"), the political differences between the various Zionist groups had shrunk further, with almost all Zionist groups seeking a Jewish state in Palestine.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGorny1987Introduction,_Chapter_8-69)[[65]](#cite_note-70) While not every Zionist group openly called for the establishment of a Jewish state in Palestine, every group in the Zionist mainstream was wedded to the idea of establishing a Jewish demographic majority there.[[66]](#cite_note-71)

In pursuing a Jewish demographic majority, the Zionist movement encountered the demographic problem posed by the presence of the local Arab population, which was predominantly non-Jewish. The practical issue of establishing a Jewish state in a majority non-Jewish region was an issue of fundamental importance for the Zionist movement.[[67]](#cite_note-72)[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFinkelstein2016Chapter_1-73) Many Zionist activists intended to establish a Jewish majority through Jewish immigration to the region.[[69]](#cite_note-74)

Zionists used the term "transfer" as a euphemism for the removal, or what would now be called [ethnic cleansing](/wiki/Ethnic_cleansing "Ethnic cleansing"), of the Palestinian population.[[70]](#cite_note-75) According to [Benny Morris](/wiki/Benny_Morris "Benny Morris"), the idea of transfer played a large role in Zionist ideology from the inception of the movement and was seen as the main method of maintaining the "Jewishness" of the Zionist's state.[[71]](#cite_note-76) He argues that "transfer" was "inevitable and inbuilt into Zionism" and that a land that was primarily Arab could not be transformed into a Jewish state without displacing the Arab population.[[72]](#cite_note-77) Further, the stability of the Jewish state could not be ensured given the Arab population's fear of displacement. He argues that this would be the primary source of conflict between the Zionist movement and the Arab population.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris199937-78)

The concept of "transfer" had a long pedigree in Zionist thought, as it was considered both moral and practical, as a way to deal with the Palestinian problem, create a Jewish homeland and avoid ethnic conflict.[[74]](#cite_note-79)[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBen-Ami200725–26-80)[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasalha2012Chapter_1-81) The concept of removing the non-Jewish population from Palestine garnered support across the entire spectrum of Zionist groups, eventually including its farthest left factions, who, after realizing the extent of the destruction of European Jewry, viewed it as a lesser evil.[[f]](#cite_note-82)[[77]](#cite_note-83) Transfer thought began early in the movement's development in various forms.[[78]](#cite_note-84) "Transfer" was seen not only as desirable but also as an ideal solution by some in the Zionist leadership, but it remained controversial.[[79]](#cite_note-85)

### Perceived deficiencies of assimilation, negation of diasporic life

Further information: [Jewish assimilation](/wiki/Jewish_assimilation "Jewish assimilation") and [Negation of the Diaspora](/wiki/Negation_of_the_Diaspora "Negation of the Diaspora")

From the perspective of some early Zionist thinkers, Jews living amongst non-Jews suffer from impediments that can be addressed only by rejecting the Jewish identity that developed [while living amongst non-Jews](/wiki/Negation_of_the_Diaspora "Negation of the Diaspora").[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEngel2021-86) Accordingly, the early Zionists sought to develop a nationalist Jewish political life in a territory where Jews constitute a demographic majority.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERabkin2006-87)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYadgar2017180,_221-88)[[83]](#cite_note-89) The early Zionist thinkers saw the integration of Jews into non-Jewish society as both unrealistic (or insufficient to address the deficiencies associated with demographic minority status) and undesirable, since assimilation was accompanied by the dilution of Jewish cultural distinctiveness.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShimoni1995Chapter_1-90) The Zionist solution to the perceived deficiencies of diasporic life (or the "[Jewish Question](/wiki/Jewish_Question "Jewish Question")") was dependent on the territorial concentration of Jews in Palestine, with the longer-term goal of establishing a Jewish demographic majority there.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGorny1987Introduction-91)[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFinkelstein2016Chapter_1-73)[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShimoni1995Chapter_1-90)

[Negation of the Diaspora](/wiki/Negation_of_the_Diaspora "Negation of the Diaspora") is a concept that asserts that Jews living in [the Diaspora](/wiki/Jewish_diaspora "Jewish diaspora")—that is, outside of the [Land of Israel](/wiki/Land_of_Israel "Land of Israel")—are in an environment that is inherently harmful and so must be fixed to ensure the survival and the cohesion of the [Jews as a people](/wiki/Jewish_peoplehood "Jewish peoplehood"), often to the extent that the Jewish people have no future without amassing at their "spiritual centre" in the Land of Israel.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchweid1996133-92)

#### Zionism and secular Jewish identity

Zionism sought to reconfigure Jewish identity and culture in nationalist and secular terms.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShimoni1995Zionism_as_Secular_Jewish_Identity-93)[[88]](#cite_note-94) According to [Yaacov Yadgar](/w/index.php?title=Yaacov_Yadgar&action=edit&redlink=1 "Yaacov Yadgar (page does not exist)"), Zionism rejected traditional Judaic definitions of what it means to be Jewish, but struggled to offer a new interpretation of Jewish identity independent of rabbinical tradition. Yagdar argues that Jewish religion is viewed as an essentially negative factor in Zionism, even in religious Zionist ideology, and as responsible for the diminishing status of Jews living as a minority.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYadgar2017Zionism,_Jewish_"Religion,"_and_Secularism-95) Responding to the challenges of modernity, Zionism sought to replace religious and community institutions with secular-nationalistic ones.[[90]](#cite_note-96) Indeed, Zionism maintained primarily the outward symbols of Jewish tradition, redefining them in a nationalistic context.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERabkin2006A_New_Identity-46)[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPenslar202318–23-97) Zionism saw itself as bringing Jews into the modern world by redefining what it means to be Jewish in terms of identification with a sovereign state, rather than Judaic faith and tradition.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAvineri2017Introduction-98)

This new identity would be based on a rejection of the life of exile. Zionism portrayed the Diaspora Jew as mentally unstable, physically frail, and prone to engaging in transient businesses. They were seen as detached from nature, purely materialistic, and focused solely on their personal gains. In contrast, the vision for the new Jew was radically different: an individual of strong moral and aesthetic values, not shackled by religion, driven by ideals and willing to challenge degrading circumstances; a liberated, dignified person eager to defend both personal and national pride.[[93]](#cite_note-99) This was the "New Jew" posited by Nordau's [Muscular Judaism](/wiki/Muscular_Judaism "Muscular Judaism") and later the pioneering "[Sabra](/wiki/Sabra_(person) "Sabra (person)")" celebrated in Israeli nationalism - not the feminine, over-intellectual, [degenerate](/wiki/Social_degeneration "Social degeneration") *Luftmenschen* (people of air) of the diaspora, as Nordau characterised them.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHaynie2009-100)[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBerg2017-101)[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMosse1992-102)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

According to Yadgar, the Zionist goal of reframing of Jewish identity in secular-nationalist terms meant primarily the decline of the status of religion in the Jewish community.[[97]](#cite_note-103) Prominent Zionist thinkers[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] frame the development of secular identity as nationalism functionally serving the same role as religion.[[98]](#cite_note-104) Zionism sought to make Jewish [ethnic-nationalism](/wiki/Ethnic_nationalism "Ethnic nationalism") the distinctive trait of Jews rather than their commitment to Judaism.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShimoni1995-105)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] According to Yadgar, Zionism instead adopted a racial understanding of Jewish identity.[[100]](#cite_note-106)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] According to Yadgar, framed this way (for instance by [A. B. Yehoshua](/wiki/A._B._Yehoshua "A. B. Yehoshua")), Jewish identity is only secondarily a matter of tradition or culture.[[101]](#cite_note-107) Nur Masalha says Zionist thinkers view the movement as a "revolt against a tradition of many centuries" of living parasitically at the margins of Western society. Indeed, he says, Zionism[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] was uncomfortable with the term "Jewish," associating it with passivity, spirituality and the stain of "galut". Instead, Zionist thinkers preferred the term "Hebrew" to describe their identity. Zionism linked the term "Jewish" with negative characteristics prevalent in European anti-Semitic stereotypes, which Zionists believed could be remedied only through sovereignty.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasalha2012Chapter_1-81)

### Revival of the Hebrew language

Main article: [Revival of the Hebrew language](/wiki/Revival_of_the_Hebrew_language "Revival of the Hebrew language")

See also: [Modern Hebrew](/wiki/Modern_Hebrew "Modern Hebrew")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Portrait_of_Eliezer_Ben-Yehuda_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Portrait_of_Eliezer_Ben-Yehuda_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_of_Eliezer_Ben-Yehuda_(cropped).jpg)

[Eliezer Ben-Yehuda](/wiki/Eliezer_Ben-Yehuda "Eliezer Ben-Yehuda") (1858–1922), founder and leader of the movement to [revive the Hebrew language](/wiki/Revival_of_the_Hebrew_language "Revival of the Hebrew language"), is considered the father of [Modern Hebrew](/wiki/Modern_Hebrew "Modern Hebrew").[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMandel200585-108)

The revival of the Hebrew language in Eastern Europe as a secular literary medium marked a significant cultural shift among Jews, who per Judaic tradition used Hebrew only for religious purposes.[[103]](#cite_note-109) The primary motivator for establishing modern Hebrew as a national language was the sense of legitimacy it gave the movement, by suggesting a connection between the Jews of ancient Israel and the Jews of the Zionist movement.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDieckhoff2003104-110) These developments are seen in Zionist historiography as a revolt against tradition, with the development of Modern Hebrew providing the basis on which a Jewish cultural renaissance might develop.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERabkin2006-87)

The [revival of the Hebrew language](/wiki/Revival_of_the_Hebrew_language "Revival of the Hebrew language") and the establishment of [Modern Hebrew](/wiki/Modern_Hebrew "Modern Hebrew") is most closely associated with the linguist [Eliezer Ben-Yehuda](/wiki/Eliezer_Ben-Yehuda "Eliezer Ben-Yehuda") and the Committee of the Hebrew Language (later replaced by the [Academy of the Hebrew Language](/wiki/Academy_of_the_Hebrew_Language "Academy of the Hebrew Language")).[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFellman20117-111)[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlau198133-112)

### Conquest of labor

Main article: [Hebrew labor](/wiki/Hebrew_labor "Hebrew labor")

In the early 20th century a more ideologically motivated wave of Zionist immigrants arrived in Palestine. With them, the Zionist movement began to emphasize the so-called "conquest of labor", the belief that the employment of exclusively Jewish labor was the pre-condition for the development of an independent Jewish society in Palestine.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGorny1987Introduction-91) The goal was to build a "pure Jewish settlement" in Palestine on the basis of "100 per cent Jewish labor" and the claim to an exclusively Jewish, highly productive economy.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlapan1979Jewish_and_Arab_Labour-113)[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShafir1996Conclusion-114) The Zionist leadership aimed to establish a fully autonomous and independent Jewish economic sector to create a new type of Jewish society. This new society was intended to reverse the traditional economic structure seen in the Jewish Diaspora, characterized by a high number of middlemen and a scarcity of productive workers. By developing fundamental sectors such as industry, agriculture, and mining, the goal was to "normalize" Jewish life that had grown "abnormal" as a result of living amongst non-Jews.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlapan1979The_Policy_of_Economic_and_Social_Separation-115) Most of the Zionist leadership saw it as imperative to employ strictly Jewish workers in order to ensure the Jewish character of the colonies. Another factor, according to [Benny Morris](/wiki/Benny_Morris "Benny Morris"), was the worry that "employment of Arabs would lead to 'Arab values' being passed on to Zionist youth and nourish the colonists' tendency to exploit and abuse their workers", as well as security concerns.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris199951-116)

The employment of exclusively Jewish labor was also intended to avoid the development of a national conflict in conjunction with a class-based conflict.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlmog19835-117) The Zionist leadership believed that by excluding Arab workers they would stimulate class conflict only within Arab society and prevent the Jewish-Arab national conflict from attaining a class dimension.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlapan1979201-118) While the Zionist settlers of the [First Aliyah](/wiki/First_Aliyah "First Aliyah") had ventured to create a "pure Jewish settlement," they did grow to rely on Arab labor due to the lack of availability of Jewish laborers during this period.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShafir1996196–200-119) With the arrival of the more ideologically driven settlers of the second aliyah, the idea of "avoda ivrit" would become more central. The future leaders of the Zionist movement saw an existential threat in the employment of Arab labor, motivating the movement to work towards a society based on purely Jewish labor.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShapira199260-120)[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShapira201445-50-121)[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris1999Chapter_2-122)

### Racial conceptions of Jewish identity

Main article: [Racial conceptions of Jewish identity in Zionism](/wiki/Racial_conceptions_of_Jewish_identity_in_Zionism "Racial conceptions of Jewish identity in Zionism")

In the late 19th century, amid attempts to apply science to notions of [race](/wiki/Race_(human_categorization) "Race (human categorization)"), some of the founders of Zionism (such as [Max Nordau](/wiki/Max_Nordau "Max Nordau")) sought to reformulate conceptions of [Jewishness](/wiki/Jewishness "Jewishness") in terms of [racial identity](/wiki/Racial_identity "Racial identity") and the ["race science" of the time](/wiki/Scientific_racism "Scientific racism"). They believed that this concept would allow them to build a new framework for collective Jewish identity,[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAvraham2017357-123) and thought that biology might provide "proof" for the "ethnonational myth of common descent" from the biblical [land of Israel](/wiki/Land_of_Israel "Land of Israel").[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHirsch2009592-124)[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDoron1980404-125) Countering [antisemitic](/wiki/Antisemitic "Antisemitic") claims that Jews were both aliens and a racially inferior people, these Zionists drew on and appropriated elements from various race theories,[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHart1999271-126)[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAvraham2013355–357-127)[[g]](#cite_note-128) to argue that only a [home for the Jewish people](/wiki/First_Zionist_Congress#Basel_Program "First Zionist Congress") could enable the physical regeneration of the Jewish people and a renaissance of pride in their ancient cultural traditions.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVogt201585–86-129) According to Nur Masalha, Zionist nationalism embraced [pan-Germanic](/wiki/Pan-Germanic "Pan-Germanic") ideologies, which stressed the concept of *das [Volk](/wiki/Volkish "Volkish")*: people of shared ancestry should pursue separation and establish a unified state.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasalha2012Chapter_1-81)

The contrasting [assimilationist viewpoint](/wiki/Jewish_assimilation "Jewish assimilation") was that Jewishness consisted in an attachment to [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") as a religion and culture. Both the [orthodox](/wiki/Orthodox_Judaism "Orthodox Judaism") and [liberal](/wiki/Reform_Judaism "Reform Judaism") establishments often rejected this idea.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEfron19944,_144–146-130)[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAvraham2013358-131)[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFalk201735–36-132) Subsequently, Zionist and non-Zionist Jews vigorously debated aspects of this proposition in terms of the merits or otherwise of [diaspora life](/wiki/Jewish_diaspora "Jewish diaspora"). While Zionism embarked on its project of social engineering in [Mandatory Palestine](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine"), [ethnonationalist](/wiki/Ethnonationalist "Ethnonationalist") politics on the European continent strengthened and, by the 1930s, some [German Jews](/wiki/German_Jews "German Jews"), acting defensively, asserted Jewish collective rights by redefining Jews as a race after [Nazism](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism") rose to power.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAvraham2013354–374-133)

With the [establishment of Israel](/wiki/Establishment_of_Israel "Establishment of Israel") in 1948, the ["ingathering of the exiles"](/wiki/Gathering_of_Israel "Gathering of Israel"), and the [Law of Return](/wiki/Law_of_Return "Law of Return"), the question of Jewish origins and biological unity came to assume particular importance during early nation building. Conscious of this, Israeli medical researchers and geneticists were careful to avoid any language that might resonate with racial ideas. Themes of "blood logic" or "race" have nevertheless been described as a recurrent feature of modern Jewish thought in both scholarship and popular belief.[[h]](#cite_note-134) Despite this, many aspects of the role of race in the formation of Zionist concepts of a Jewish identity were rarely addressed until recently.[[127]](#cite_note-135)

## Historical and religious background

See also: [Jewish history](/wiki/Jewish_history "Jewish history"), [History of Israel](/wiki/History_of_Israel "History of Israel"), [History of Palestine](/wiki/History_of_Palestine "History of Palestine"), [History of the Jews and Judaism in the Land of Israel](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_and_Judaism_in_the_Land_of_Israel "History of the Jews and Judaism in the Land of Israel"), and [Jewish peoplehood](/wiki/Jewish_peoplehood "Jewish peoplehood")

The [concept of the "return"](/wiki/Return_to_Zion "Return to Zion") was a powerful symbol within religious Jewish belief,[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShapira2004-136)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[129]](#cite_note-137)[[130]](#cite_note-138)[[131]](#cite_note-139) traditionally emphasizing that their return should be determined by Divine Providence rather than human action.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAvineri2017-140) The cultural memory of Jews in the diaspora revered the Land of Israel. Religious tradition held that a future [messianic age](/wiki/Messianic_Age#Judaism "Messianic Age") would usher in their return as a people,[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor197110,_11-141) a 'return to Zion' commemorated particularly at [Passover](/wiki/Passover "Passover") and in [Yom Kippur](/wiki/Yom_Kippur "Yom Kippur") prayers.[[i]](#cite_note-142) The biblical prophecy of [*Kibbutz Galuyot*](/wiki/Gathering_of_Israel "Gathering of Israel"), the ingathering of exiles in the Land of Israel as foretold by the [Prophets](/wiki/Nevi%27im "Nevi'im"), became a central idea in Zionism.[[134]](#cite_note-143)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[135]](#cite_note-144)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERussellGordonAmerica1917-145) The transformation of this religious, and primarily passive connection between Jews and Palestine, into an active, secular, nationalist movement arose in the context of ideological developments within modern European nations in the 19th century. According to [Gideon Shimoni](/w/index.php?title=Gideon_Shimoni&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gideon Shimoni (page does not exist)"), the religious Judaic notion of being a nation was distinct from the modern European notion of nationalism.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShimoni1995-105)

### Proto-national characteristics in ancient Jewish identity

Multiple scholars have argued that Jewish identity in antiquity exhibited national or [proto-national](/wiki/Proto-nationalism "Proto-nationalism") characteristics. Political scientist [Tom Garvin](/wiki/Tom_Garvin "Tom Garvin") described ancient Jewish identity as "something strangely like modern nationalism."[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarvin199367-146) [Anthony D. Smith](/wiki/Anthony_D._Smith "Anthony D. Smith") wrote that Jews during the late Second Temple period closely approximated the modern concept of a nation.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith199348–50-147) Similarly, [Adrian Hastings](/wiki/Adrian_Hastings "Adrian Hastings") referred to the Jews as the "true proto-nation."[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHastings1997186–187-148)

Historians have identified elements within the [Jewish–Roman wars](/wiki/Jewish%E2%80%93Roman_wars "Jewish–Roman wars") that they describe as expressions of ancient nationalism, and some have drawn analogies with modern Zionism. The [Great Jewish Revolt](/wiki/Great_Jewish_Revolt "Great Jewish Revolt") against Roman rule (66–73 CE) is frequently cited as a major example of ancient Jewish nationalism.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodblatt20063,_87–88-149) Historian David Goodblatt draws parallels between the revolt and modern national liberation movements, citing the adoption of coins inscribed with "Israel" and the use of a dating system based on the "freedom of Israel" era.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodblatt200688-150) According to historian [Doron Mendels](/wiki/Doron_Mendels "Doron Mendels"), the rebels' nationalist sentiment was reinforced by collective memory of the earlier [Maccabean revolt](/wiki/Maccabean_revolt "Maccabean revolt"), which had resulted in a period of Jewish sovereignty under the [Hasmonean kingdom](/wiki/Hasmonean_kingdom "Hasmonean kingdom")—a time during which Jewish national consciousness expanded.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMendels199226-151) Goodblatt also compares the rebels' use of the term "Zion" to rally popular support on their coins has been compared to modern Zionism.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodblatt2006203-152) The later [Diaspora Revolt](/wiki/Diaspora_Revolt "Diaspora Revolt") (115–117 CE) is believed to have been motivated, in part, by aspirations among Jewish communities to return to Judaea and rebuild Jerusalem and the Temple,[[144]](#cite_note-EC-153) an impulse that historian [E. Mary Smallwood](/wiki/E._Mary_Smallwood "E. Mary Smallwood") likened to an ancient form of Zionism.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmallwood2001397-154)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Monnaie_-_Prutah%2C_bronze%2C_J%C3%A9rusalem%2C_Jud%C3%A9e%2C_premi%C3%A8re_guerre_jud%C3%A9o-romaine_-_btv1b11318591v_%282_of_2%29.jpg/250px-Monnaie_-_Prutah%2C_bronze%2C_J%C3%A9rusalem%2C_Jud%C3%A9e%2C_premi%C3%A8re_guerre_jud%C3%A9o-romaine_-_btv1b11318591v_%282_of_2%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Monnaie_-_Prutah,_bronze,_J%C3%A9rusalem,_Jud%C3%A9e,_premi%C3%A8re_guerre_jud%C3%A9o-romaine_-_btv1b11318591v_(2_of_2).jpg)

Coin bearing the inscription "Freedom of Zion," minted by Jewish rebels during the [Great Jewish Revolt](/wiki/Great_Jewish_Revolt "Great Jewish Revolt") (66–73 CE), whose use of Zion as a rallying symbol has been compared to modern Zionism

The final large-scale Jewish uprising against Roman rule, the [Bar Kokhba revolt](/wiki/Bar_Kokhba_revolt "Bar Kokhba revolt") (132–135 CE), temporarily restored Jewish control over parts of Judaea but was ultimately crushed by the Romans, resulting in extensive casualties, enslavement, and widespread displacement of Jews. Following the Jewish–Roman wars, active expressions of Jewish nationalism (such as armed resistance and attempts to restore political sovereignty) largely ceased. However, scholars argue that Jewish national identity persisted in a more passive form, surviving in collective memory and religious tradition. Jewish law, language, and cultural practices continued to reinforce a sense of peoplehood, preserving national consciousness in exile and dispersion.[[146]](#cite_note-EC1-155) According to Adrian Hastings, the persistence of Jewish national awareness (rather than merely an ethnic identity) made it possible for modern Zionism to emerge when new political conditions arose.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHastings1997186–187-148)

### Forerunners of Zionism

See also: [Aliyah § Middle Ages](/wiki/Aliyah#Middle_Ages "Aliyah")

The forerunners of Zionism, rather than being causally connected to the later development of Zionism, are thinkers and activists who expressed some notion of Jewish national consciousness or advocated for the migration of Jews to Palestine. These attempts were not continuous as national movements typically are.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPenslar202325-156)[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShimoni1995Chapter_2-157) The most notable proto-Zionists were [rabbis](/wiki/Rabbi "Rabbi") such as [Judah Alkalai](/wiki/Judah_Alkalai "Judah Alkalai") and [Zvi Hirsch Kalischer](/wiki/Zvi_Hirsch_Kalischer "Zvi Hirsch Kalischer").[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDieckhoff2003Political_Beginnings_of_Zionism-158) Their idea of Jews as a collective was strongly tied to religious notions distinct from the secular movement referred to as Zionism that developed at the end of the century.[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPenslar202327–29-159)

In contrast, [Moses Hess](/wiki/Moses_Hess "Moses Hess"), who is regarded as the first modern Jewish nationalist, advocated for the establishment of an independent Jewish state in pursuit of the economic and social normalization of the Jewish people.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESela2002Zionism-160) Hess believed that emancipation alone was not a sufficient solution to the problems faced by European Jewry.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShimoni1995Chapter_2-157)

Christian restorationist ideas promoting the migration of Jews to Palestine contributed to the ideological and historical context that gave a sense of credibility to these pre-Zionist initiatives.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShimoni1995Chapter_2-157) Restorationist ideas were a prerequisite for the success of Zionism, since although it was created by Jews, Zionism was dependent on support from Christians, although it is unclear how much Christian ideas influenced the early Zionists. Zionism was also dependent on the thinkers of the *[Haskalah](/wiki/Haskalah "Haskalah")* or Jewish enlightenment, such as [Peretz Smolenskin](/wiki/Peretz_Smolenskin "Peretz Smolenskin") in 1872, although it often depicted it as its opponent.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPenslar202327-161)

## History

Main article: [History of Zionism](/wiki/History_of_Zionism "History of Zionism")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Zionism](/wiki/Timeline_of_Zionism "Timeline of Zionism").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Memorandum_to_Protestant_Monarchs_of_Europe_for_the_restoration_of_the_Jews_to_Palestine%2C_Colonial_Times_1841.jpg/250px-Memorandum_to_Protestant_Monarchs_of_Europe_for_the_restoration_of_the_Jews_to_Palestine%2C_Colonial_Times_1841.jpg)](/wiki/File:Memorandum_to_Protestant_Monarchs_of_Europe_for_the_restoration_of_the_Jews_to_Palestine,_Colonial_Times_1841.jpg)

"Memorandum to the Protestant Powers of the North of Europe and America", published in the *[Colonial Times](/wiki/Colonial_Times "Colonial Times")* (Hobart, Tasmania, Australia), in 1841

### Jewish nationalism and emancipation

See also: [Haskalah](/wiki/Haskalah "Haskalah")

Ideas of Jewish cultural unity developed a specifically political expression in the 1860s as Jewish intellectuals began promoting the idea of Jewish nationalism. This emerged amid the late [19th century European](/wiki/Rise_of_nationalism_in_Europe "Rise of nationalism in Europe") trend of [national revivals](/wiki/National_revival "National revival").[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeininStein2006157-162)[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKagarlitsky2014[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidcf3pAwAAQBAJpgPA294_294]-163)

Zionism emerged towards the end of the "best century"[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAvineri2017Introduction-98) for Jews who for the first time were allowed as equals into European society and gained access to schools, universities, and professions that were previously closed to them.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAvineri2017Introduction-98) By the 1870s, Jews had achieved almost complete [civic emancipation](/wiki/Jewish_emancipation "Jewish emancipation") in all the states of western and central Europe.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShimoni1995-105) By 1914, Jews had moved from the margins to the forefront of European society. In the urban centers of Europe and America, Jews played an influential role in professional and intellectual life.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAvineri2017Introduction-98) During this period, as [Jewish assimilation](/wiki/Jewish_assimilation "Jewish assimilation") was still progressing most promisingly, some Jewish intellectuals and religious traditionalists framed assimilation as a humiliating negation of Jewish cultural distinctiveness.[[155]](#cite_note-164) The development of Zionism and other Jewish nationalist movements grew out of these sentiments.[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShimoni1995Ethnicity_and_Nationalism-165) In this sense, Zionism can be read as a response to the Haskala and the challenges of modernity and liberalism, rather than purely a response to antisemitism.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAvineri2017Introduction-98)

Emancipation in Eastern Europe progressed more slowly,[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoldberg200920-166) to the point that Deickoff writes "social conditions were such that they made the idea of individual assimilation pointless". Antisemitism, pogroms and official policies in Tsarist Russia led to the emigration of three million Jews in the years between 1882 and 1914, only 1% of which went to Palestine. Those who went to Palestine were driven primarily by ideas of self-determination and Jewish identity, rather than just in response to pogroms or economic insecurity.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAvineri2017Introduction-98) Zionism's emergence in the late 19th century was among assimilated Central European Jews who, despite their formal emancipation, still felt excluded from high society. Many of these Jews had moved away from traditional religious observances and were largely secular, mirroring a broader trend of secularization in Europe. Despite their efforts to integrate, the Jews of Central and Eastern Europe were frustrated by continued lack of acceptance by the local national movements that tended toward intolerance and exclusivity.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERabkin2006Orientations-167) For the early Zionists, if nationalism posed a challenge to European Jewry, it also proposed a solution.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShlaim2001Introduction-168)

### Leon Pinsker, Theodor Herzl and the birth of modern political Zionism

After a new wave of [Russian pogroms](/wiki/Russian_pogroms "Russian pogroms"), the former assimilationist [Leon Pinsker](/wiki/Leon_Pinsker "Leon Pinsker") concluded that the root of the Jewish problem was that Jews formed a distinctive element that could not be assimilated.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShimoni1995-105) For Pinsker, emancipation could not resolve the problems of the Jewish people.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESela2002-169) In Pinsker's analysis, antisemitism was primarily driven by Jews' lack of a homeland. The solution Pinsker proposed in his 1882 pamphlet *[Autoemancipation](/wiki/Autoemancipation "Autoemancipation")* was for Jews to become a "normal" nation and acquire a homeland over which they would have sovereignty.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAvineri2017Introduction-98)[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESela2002Zionism-160) Pinsker primarily viewed Jewish emigration a solution for dealing with the "surplus of Jews, the inassimilable residue" from Eastern Europe who had arrived in Germany in response to the pogroms.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShafir1996-170)[[j]](#cite_note-172)

The pogroms motivated a small number of Jews to establish groups in the [Pale of Settlement](/wiki/Pale_of_Settlement "Pale of Settlement") (a region in western Russia) and [Poland](/wiki/Congress_Poland "Congress Poland"), aimed at supporting Jewish emigration to Palestine. The publication of *Autoemancipation* provided these groups with an ideological charter around which they were confederated into [Hibbat Zion](/wiki/Lovers_of_Zion "Lovers of Zion") ("Lovers of Zion") in 1887, where Pinsker took a leading role.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris1999Palestine_on_the_Eve-173) The settlements established by Hibbat Zion lacked sufficient funds and were ultimately not very successful but are seen as the first of several aliyahs, or waves of settlement, that led to the eventual establishment of the state of Israel.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGorny1987The_Overt_Question,_1882–1917-174) The conditions in Eastern Europe eventually provided Zionism with a base of Jews seeking to overcome the challenges of external ostracism, from the Tsarist regime, and internal changes within the Jewish communities there.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDieckhoff200350-175) The groups that formed Hibbat Zion included the [Bilu](/wiki/Bilu_(movement) "Bilu (movement)") group, which established its first settlement in 1882.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShapira201229-176) [Anita Shapira](/wiki/Anita_Shapira "Anita Shapira") describes the Bilu as serving the role of a prototype for the settlement groups that followed.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShapira201432-33-177) At the end of the 19th century, Jews remained a small minority in Palestine.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris200147-178)

A key event that put the modern Zionist movement in motion was the [Dreyfus affair](/wiki/Dreyfus_affair "Dreyfus affair"), which erupted in France in 1894.[[169]](#cite_note-179)[[170]](#cite_note-Cohn-1970-180)[[171]](#cite_note-Mayorek-1994-181) The case had a profound impact on Central and Western European Jews.[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris200120–21-182) Among those who witnessed the event was Herzl.[[170]](#cite_note-Cohn-1970-180)[[171]](#cite_note-Mayorek-1994-181)

At this point, Zionism remained a scattered movement. In the 1890s, Herzl infused Zionism with a practical urgency and worked to unify the various strands of the movement.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasalha2018-183)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] The title of Herzl's 1896 manifesto providing the ideological basis for Zionism, *[Der Judenstaat](/wiki/Der_Judenstaat "Der Judenstaat")*, is typically translated as The Jewish State. Herzl sought to establish a state where Jews would be the majority and as a result, politically dominant.[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDieckhoff2003[httpswwwgooglecombookseditionThe_Invention_of_a_Nation1m_w5J0X7lUChlengbpv1pgPA39printsecfrontcoverdq22Der20Judenstaat22_39]-184) [Ahad Ha'am](/wiki/Ahad_Ha%27am "Ahad Ha'am"), the founder of cultural Zionism, criticized the lack of Jewish cultural activity and creativity in Herzl's envisioned state, which Ha'am referred to as "the state of the Jews". He pointed to the envisioned European and German culture of the state where Jews were simply the transmitters of imperialist culture rather than producers or creators of culture.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasalha2012Chapter_1-81) Like Pinsker, Herzl saw antisemitism as a reality that could only be addressed by the territorial concentration of Jews in a Jewish state.[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasalha2014Introduction-185)

Herzl's project was purely secular; the selection of Palestine, after considering other locations, was motivated by the credibility the name would give to the movement.[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasalha2014Introduction-185) From early on, Herzl recognized that Zionism could not succeed without the support of a [great power](/wiki/Great_power "Great power").[[176]](#cite_note-186) He hoped that his state would support the great powers' interests and would "form part of a defensive wall for Europe in Asia, an outpost of civilization against barbarism."[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris199923-187)

Herzl's efforts led to the [First Zionist Congress](/wiki/First_Zionist_Congress "First Zionist Congress") at [Basel](/wiki/Basel "Basel") in 1897, which created the Zionist Organization (ZO), renamed in 1960 as World Zionist Organization (WZO),[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESethi2007-188) and adopted the [Basel Program](/wiki/Basel_Program "Basel Program"), which codified the official objective of establishing a legally recognized home for the Jewish people in Palestine.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasalha2018-183) The Zionist Organization was to be the main administrative body of the movement and would go on to establish the [Jewish Colonial Trust](/wiki/Jewish_Colonial_Trust "Jewish Colonial Trust"), whose objectives were to encourage European Jewish emigration to Palestine and to assist with the economic development of the colonies.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Territories considered

Main articles: [Jewish territorialism](/wiki/Jewish_territorialism "Jewish territorialism") and [Proposals for a Jewish state](/wiki/Proposals_for_a_Jewish_state "Proposals for a Jewish state")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/JewishChronicle1896.jpg/330px-JewishChronicle1896.jpg)](/wiki/File:JewishChronicle1896.jpg)

Front page of *[The Jewish Chronicle](/wiki/The_Jewish_Chronicle "The Jewish Chronicle")*, January 17, 1896, showing an article by Theodor Herzl, a month prior to the publication of his pamphlet *[Der Judenstaat](/wiki/Der_Judenstaat "Der Judenstaat")*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/THEODOR_HERZL_AT_THE_FIRST_ZIONIST_CONGRESS_IN_BASEL_ON_25.8.1897._%D7%AA%D7%90%D7%95%D7%93%D7%95%D7%A8_%D7%94%D7%A8%D7%A6%D7%9C_%D7%91%D7%A7%D7%95%D7%A0%D7%92%D7%A8%D7%A1_%D7%94%D7%A6%D7%99%D7%95%D7%A0%D7%99_%D7%94%D7%A8%D7%90%D7%A9%D7%95%D7%9F_-_1897.8.25_%28cropped%29.jpg/500px-THEODOR_HERZL_AT_THE_FIRST_ZIONIST_CONGRESS_IN_BASEL_ON_25.8.1897._%D7%AA%D7%90%D7%95%D7%93%D7%95%D7%A8_%D7%94%D7%A8%D7%A6%D7%9C_%D7%91%D7%A7%D7%95%D7%A0%D7%92%D7%A8%D7%A1_%D7%94%D7%A6%D7%99%D7%95%D7%A0%D7%99_%D7%94%D7%A8%D7%90%D7%A9%D7%95%D7%9F_-_1897.8.25_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:THEODOR_HERZL_AT_THE_FIRST_ZIONIST_CONGRESS_IN_BASEL_ON_25.8.1897._%D7%AA%D7%90%D7%95%D7%93%D7%95%D7%A8_%D7%94%D7%A8%D7%A6%D7%9C_%D7%91%D7%A7%D7%95%D7%A0%D7%92%D7%A8%D7%A1_%D7%94%D7%A6%D7%99%D7%95%D7%A0%D7%99_%D7%94%D7%A8%D7%90%D7%A9%D7%95%D7%9F_-_1897.8.25_(cropped).jpg)

The delegates at the First Zionist Congress, held in [Basel](/wiki/Basel "Basel"), Switzerland (1897)

Throughout the first decade of the Zionist movement, Herzl and other Zionist figures considered locations for a Jewish state outside of the Land of Israel, such as ["Uganda"](/wiki/Uganda_Scheme "Uganda Scheme") (actually parts of [British East Africa](/wiki/British_East_Africa "British East Africa") that are today in [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya")), [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina"), [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus"), [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"), [Mozambique](/wiki/Mozambique "Mozambique"), and the [Sinai Peninsula](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula").[[179]](#cite_note-189) Herzl was initially content with any Jewish self-governed state.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAvivShneer2005[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidkdBtob8RWEMCqzionismugandaargentinapgPA10_10]-190) Jewish settlement of Argentina was the project of [Maurice de Hirsch](/wiki/Maurice_de_Hirsch "Maurice de Hirsch").[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHazony2000150-191) It is unclear if Herzl seriously considered this alternative plan;[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriedman2021239–240-192) however, he later affirmed that only Palestine could gain enough Jewish support because of the historic ties of Jews with that area.[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerzl189629_(31)-193)[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShapira201223-194) A major concern and driving reason for considering other territories was the Russian pogroms, in particular the [Kishinev](/wiki/Kishinev_pogrom "Kishinev pogrom") massacre, and the resulting need for quick resettlement in a safer place.[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHazony2000369-195)

### Early Zionist settlement

Main article: [First Aliyah](/wiki/First_Aliyah "First Aliyah")

In the early twentieth century, Zionism advanced by establishing towns, colonies, and an independent monetary system in Palestine. Due to the unstable local economy and fluctuating currency values under Ottoman rule, Zionists created their own financial institutions. Despite their small numbers, the Zionists instilled a fear of territorial displacement in the local population,[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPappé2004Chapter_2-196) which led to Palestinian resistance and the settlers' eventual use of military force.[[187]](#cite_note-197) Initially, the impact on rural Palestinians was minimal, with only a few villages encountering Jewish colonies. However, after [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and as Zionist land purchases increased, the rural population began to experience dramatic changes. From almost the beginning of Zionist settlement, Palestinians viewed Zionism as an expansionist endeavor. According to Israeli historian Benny Morris, Zionism was inherently expansionist and always had the goal of turning the entirety of Palestine into a Jewish state. In addition, Morris describes the Zionists as intent on politically and physically dispossessing the Arabs.[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris1999Conclusions-198) Early warnings from local leaders in the 1880s about the destabilizing effects of Jewish immigration went largely unheeded until these later developments.[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPappé2004The_Arrival_of_Zionism-199) By the early 20th century, there were fourteen Zionist settlements in Palestine, established through land purchases from both local and external landowners.[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPappé2004The_Arrival_of_Zionism-199)

From the outset, the Zionist leadership saw land acquisition as essential to achieving their goal of establishing a Jewish state. This acquisition was strategic, aiming to create a continuous area of Jewish land. The WZO established the [Jewish National Fund](/wiki/Jewish_National_Fund "Jewish National Fund") (JNF) in 1901, with the stated goal "to redeem the land of Palestine as the inalienable possession of the Jewish people." The notion of land "redemption" entailed that the land could not be sold and could not be leased to a non-Jew nor should the land be worked by Arabs,[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQuigley20054–7-200) though most Zionists continued to employ [fellaheen](/wiki/Fellaheen "Fellaheen") to perform labor on their lands.[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnderson201384-201) The land purchased was primarily from absentee landlords, and upon purchase of the land, the tenant farmers who traditionally had rights of usufruct were often expelled.[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhalidi2010102-202) Herzl publicly opposed this dispossession, but wrote privately in his diary: "We shall try to spirit the penniless population across the border by procuring employment for it in the transit countries, while denying it any employment in our country... Both the process of expropriation and the removal of the poor must be carried out discreetly and circumspectly."[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris199920–24-203) The arrival of Zionist settlers to Palestine in the late 19th century is widely seen as the start of the [Israeli–Palestinian conflict](/wiki/Israeli%E2%80%93Palestinian_conflict "Israeli–Palestinian conflict").[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasalha201270-11)[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKarsh200912-12)[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris20081-13) Zionists wanted to create a Jewish state in Palestine with as much land, as many Jews, and as few Palestinian Arabs as possible.[[4]](#cite_note-ZionistLandJewsArabs1-5)[[5]](#cite_note-ZionistLandJewsArabs2-6)

In 1903, 'the Eretz Israel assembly' was held by [Menachem Ussishkin](/wiki/Menachem_Ussishkin "Menachem Ussishkin"). This assembly marked the beginning of a more formalized Zionist colonization effort. Under his leadership, both professional and political organizations were established, paving the way for a sustained Zionist presence in the region.[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPappé2004The_Arrival_of_Zionism-199) Ussishkin delineated three methods for the Zionist movement to acquire land: by force and conquest, by expropriation via governmental authority, and by purchase. The only option available to the movement at the moment in his perspective was the last one, "until at some point we become rulers".[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris199935-40-204)

#### Second Aliyah

Main article: [Second Aliyah](/wiki/Second_Aliyah "Second Aliyah")

The second wave of Zionist settlement came with the [Second Aliyah](/wiki/Second_Aliyah "Second Aliyah") starting in 1904. The settlers of this period laid the foundational elements for the Jewish society in Palestine envisioned by the Zionist movement. They established the first two political parties, the socialist [Po'alei Zion](/wiki/Po%27alei_Zion "Po'alei Zion") and the non-socialist [Ha-Po'el Ha-Tza'ir](/wiki/Hapoel_Hatzair "Hapoel Hatzair") and initiated the first collective agricultural settlements known as [kibbutzim](/wiki/Kibbutzim "Kibbutzim"), which were fundamental in the formation of the Israeli state.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShafir1996-170) They also formed the first underground military group, [Ha-Shomer](/wiki/Ha-Shomer "Ha-Shomer"), which later evolved into the Haganah and eventually became the core of the Israeli army. Many leaders of the Zionist national movement were products of the Second Aliyah.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGorny1987-205) The Zionists of the second aliyah were also more ideologically motivated than those of the first aliyah. In particular, they sought the "[conquest of labor](/wiki/Conquest_of_labor "Conquest of labor")", which entailed the exclusion of Arabs from the labor market.[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShafir1996"Conquest_of_Labor"-206)

### The Balfour Declaration and World War I

Main articles: [Balfour Declaration](/wiki/Balfour_Declaration "Balfour Declaration"), [Mandate for Palestine](/wiki/Mandate_for_Palestine "Mandate for Palestine"), and [Homeland for the Jewish people](/wiki/Homeland_for_the_Jewish_people "Homeland for the Jewish people")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Palestine_claimed_by_WZO_1919.png/250px-Palestine_claimed_by_WZO_1919.png)](/wiki/File:Palestine_claimed_by_WZO_1919.png)

Palestine as claimed by the World Zionist Organization in 1919 at the [Paris Peace Conference](/wiki/Paris_Peace_Conference,_1919 "Paris Peace Conference, 1919")

## World War I period

While there were 85,000 Jews living in Palestine in 1914, that number went down to 56,000 by 1917. The Arab population also suffered greatly during this time.[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShapira201267-207) When the Ottoman Empire entered the war on Germany's side in October 1914, tens of thousands of Russian Jews became enemy citizens, and many opted to leave rather than become Ottoman citizens and be subject to conscription.[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShapira201267-207) This gave the Ottomans an opportunity to suppress the Zionist movement.[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShapira201267-207) The war also had a negative effect on trade in Yishuv.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShapira201267-68-208) On January 17, 1914, the Ottomans announced, with no prior warning, that all foreign nationals must immediately board a ship in Jaffa to be taken to Alexandria.[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShapira201268-209) In April 1917, the Ottomans orders all Jews to leave Jaffa.[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShapira201268-209) In October 1917, after the Ottomans discovered that [Nili](/wiki/Nili "Nili") had been giving intelligence to the British, they arrested people at random and imposed a curfew as collective punishment.[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShapira201269-210) Most Zionists came to the conclusion that there was no hope of change under the Ottomans and therefore supported the British conquest of Palestine, and they viewed the British as redeemers.[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShapira201269-70-211)

At the start of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the Zionist leadership attempted to persuade the British government of the benefits of sponsoring a Jewish colony in Palestine. Their main initial success was in establishing a lobbying group centered around the [Rothschild family](/wiki/Rothschild_family "Rothschild family"), largely driven by [Chaim Weizmann](/wiki/Chaim_Weizmann "Chaim Weizmann").[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShlaim2001-212)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] In the 1917 [Balfour Declaration](/wiki/Balfour_Declaration "Balfour Declaration"), Britain declared its support for "the establishment in Palestine of a national home for the Jewish people". The declaration was largely motivated by war-time considerations and antisemitic preconceptions about the putative influence Jews had on the [Tsarist government](/wiki/Tsarist_autocracy "Tsarist autocracy") and in the shaping of American policy.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShapira2014The_Balfour_Declaration-213)[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPappé2004Palestine_in_the_First_World_War-214) Though his decision was also motivated by religious convictions,[[k]](#cite_note-216) Balfour himself had passed the [Aliens Act 1905](/wiki/Aliens_Act_1905 "Aliens Act 1905"), which aimed to keep Eastern European Jews out of Britain.[[l]](#cite_note-218) More decisive were Britain's colonial and imperial geopolitical goals in the region, specifically in retaining control over the [Suez Canal](/wiki/Suez_Canal "Suez Canal") by establishing a pro-British state in the region.[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShapira201470-71-219)[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoy201633-35-220) Weizmann's role in obtaining the Balfour Declaration led to his election as the Zionist movement's leader. He remained in that role until 1948, and then was elected as the first [President of Israel](/wiki/President_of_Israel "President of Israel") after the nation gained independence.

For Weizmann, Palestine was a Jewish and not an Arab country. The state he sought at that period would stretch to the east of the Jordan River and extend to the [Litani River](/wiki/Litani_River "Litani River") (a river in Lebanon, then part of the Ottoman Empire). Weizmann's strategy involved incrementally approaching this goal over a long period, in the form of settlement and land acquisition.[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlapan1979[httpswwwgooglecombookseditionZionism_and_the_PalestinianscJRtAAAAMAAJhlengbpv1bsq22Greater20Israel22_18]-221)[[210]](#cite_note-222) Weizmann was open to the idea of Arabs and Jews jointly running Palestine through an elected council with equal representation, but he did not view the Arabs as equal partners in negotiations about the country's future. In particular, he was steadfast in his view of the "moral superiority" of the Jewish claim to Palestine over the Arab claim and believed these negotiations should be conducted solely between Britain and the Jews.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShlaim2001-212) According to Zionist Israeli historian Simha Flapan, the essential assumptions of Weizmann's strategy were later adopted by [David Ben-Gurion](/wiki/David_Ben-Gurion "David Ben-Gurion") and subsequent Zionist leaders.[[211]](#cite_note-223)

### King–Crane Commission

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/King_Crane_Commission_1919_Summary_of_Arguments_Presented_to_the_Commission_For_and_Against_Zionism.jpg/250px-King_Crane_Commission_1919_Summary_of_Arguments_Presented_to_the_Commission_For_and_Against_Zionism.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_Crane_Commission_1919_Summary_of_Arguments_Presented_to_the_Commission_For_and_Against_Zionism.jpg)

During the [1919 Paris Peace Conference](/wiki/1919_Paris_Peace_Conference "1919 Paris Peace Conference"), an [Inter-Allied Commission](/wiki/King%E2%80%93Crane_Commission "King–Crane Commission") was sent to Palestine to assess the views of the local population; the report summarized the arguments received from petitioners for and against Zionism.

In 1919, the US-based [King–Crane Commission](/wiki/King%E2%80%93Crane_Commission "King–Crane Commission") started with a strongly sympathetic disposition towards Zionism but concluded that the maximum Zionist demands implied subjection of Palestinians to Jewish rule and that this was a violation of the principle of self-determination, given the anti-Zionist sentiment of the non-Jewish population.[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOvendale2015[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidXM_MCgAAQBAJpgPA51_51–]-224) The report stated that "The initial claim, often submitted by Zionist representatives, that they have a 'right' to Palestine based on occupation of two thousand years ago, can barely be seriously considered."[[213]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQuigley2021181-225)[[214]](#cite_note-Crane-226) Consequently, it recommended a considerably "modified" or "reduced" version of the Zionist programme, with Palestine as a Jewish national home but not a Jewish state.[[214]](#cite_note-Crane-226)

### Beginning of Revisionist Zionism

[Ze'ev Jabotinsky](/wiki/Ze%27ev_Jabotinsky "Ze'ev Jabotinsky") founded the [Revisionist Party](/wiki/Revisionist_Zionism "Revisionist Zionism") in 1925, which took on a more militant ethos and openly maximalist agenda than Weizmann and Ben-Gurion. Jabotinsky rejected Weizmann's strategy of incremental state building, instead preferring to immediately declare sovereignty over the entire region, which extended to both the East and West bank of the Jordan river.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShlaim2001-212) Like Weizmann and Herzl, Jabotinsky also believed that the support of a Great Power was essential to the success of Zionism. From early on, Jabotinksy openly rejected the possibility of a "voluntary agreement" with the Arabs of Palestine. He instead believed in building an "[iron wall](/wiki/Iron_Wall_(essay) "Iron Wall (essay)")" of Jewish military force to break Arab resistance to Zionism, at which point an agreement could be established.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShlaim2001-212) Jabotinsky's "iron wall" strategy would have a lasting effect on the Zionist perspective towards the demographic problem posed by the presence of the local Palestinian population.[[215]](#cite_note-227)[[216]](#cite_note-228) Both the left and right factions of Zionism would rely on this strategy of leveraging military strength in pursuit of political aspirations.[[217]](#cite_note-229)

### British Mandate and development of the Zionist quasi-state

After the war, the plan for a greater Arab kingdom under the Hashemite family was abandoned when King Faisal was [expelled from Damascus](/wiki/1920_capture_of_Damascus "1920 capture of Damascus") by the French in 1920. In parallel, the Zionist demand for a clear British acknowledgment of the entirety of Palestine as the Jewish national home was rejected. Instead, Britain committed only to establishing a Jewish national home "in Palestine" and promised to facilitate this without prejudicing the rights of existing "non-Jewish communities"—these qualifying statements aroused the concern of Zionist leaders at the time.[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGorny1987Historical_Background-230)

The British mandate over Palestine, established in 1922, was based on the Balfour Declaration, explicitly privileging the Jewish minority over the Arab majority.[[219]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhalidi202037–40-231) In addition to declaring British support for the establishment of a "Jewish national home" in Palestine, the mandate included provisions facilitating Jewish immigration, and granting the Zionist movement the status of representing Jewish national interests.[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGorny1987Historical_Background-230) In particular, the Jewish Agency, the embodiment of the Zionist movement in Palestine, was made a partner of the mandatory government, acquiring international diplomatic status and representing Zionist interests before the League of Nations and other international venues.[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhalidi2020Chapter_1-232)

The British mandate effectively established a Jewish quasi-state in Palestine, lacking only full sovereignty. This lack of sovereignty was crucial for Zionism at this early stage, as the Jewish population was too small to defend itself against the Arabs of Palestine. The British presence provided a necessary safeguard for Jewish nationalism. To achieve political independence, Jews needed Britain's support, particularly in land purchase and immigration.[[221]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDieckhoff20037–8,_42-233) Following the Balfour Declaration, Jewish immigration to Palestine grew from 9,149 immigrants in 1921 to 33,801 in 1925—by the end of the mandate period, the Jewish population in Palestine nearly tripled, eventually reaching one third of the country's population.[[222]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoy2016Political_Background_to_the_British_Mandate_Period_(1917–1948)-234)

The mandatory administration implemented policies that favored the development of the capitalist sector, predominantly associated with the Jewish community, while disadvantaging the Arab non-capitalist sector. Between 1933 and 1937, government spending was concentrated in two main areas—development and economic services, and defense—with the former focusing on infrastructural improvements (such as railways, roads, bridges, and other public works) that were particularly beneficial for capitalist production.[[223]](#cite_note-235) In contrast to the Jewish population, the Arabs did not benefit from any government protections such as social security, employment benefits, trade union protection, job security and training opportunities. Arab wages were one third of their Jewish counterparts (including when paid by the same employer).[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoy2016British_Government_Policies-236) The mandate also included an article describing self-governing institutions intended only for the Jewish population of Palestine. No similar support or recognition was provided to the Arab majority during the time of the mandate.[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhalidi2020Chapter_1-232)
By enabling the Zionist institutions to serve as a parallel government to the Mandate, the British facilitated the separation of the economy and legitimized their quasi-state status. Accordingly, these institutions, which purported to act in the interests of Jews everywhere, were able to funnel resources into the Jewish sector in Palestine, heavily subsidizing the dominant Jewish economy.[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoy2016British_Government_Policies-236) The nucleus of the Jewish quasi-state was the [Histadrut](/wiki/Histadrut "Histadrut"), established in 1920 as an independent social, political and economic institution.[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESternhell1999Introduction-237)[[m]](#cite_note-239) The Histadrut also exercised significant control over the [Haganah](/wiki/Haganah "Haganah"), a Jewish defense force formed in 1920 in reaction to Arab riots. Originally created to defend the community, Haganah evolved into a permanent underground reserve army fully integrated into the Jewish political structure. Although the British authorities disapproved of the Haganah, particularly its method of stealing arms from British bases, they did not disband it.[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECleveland2010252-240) The Histadrut operated as a completely independent entity, without interference from the British mandate authorities. Ben-Gurion saw the Histadrut's detachment from socialist ideology to be one of its key strengths; indeed it was the General Organization of Workers in Israel. In particular, the Histadrut worked towards national unity and aimed to dominate the capitalist system en route to gaining political power, not to create a socialist utopia.[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESternhell1999Ends_and_Means:_The_Labor_Ideology_and_the_Histadrut-241)

As secretary general of the Histadrut and leader of the Zionist labor movement, Ben-Gurion adopted similar strategies and objectives as Weizmann during this period, disagreeing primarily on issues of specific tactical moves up until 1939.[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlapan1979131-242) The middle class grew dramatically in size with the arrival of the fourth aliyah in 1924, motivating a political shift within the labor movement.[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDieckhoff200391-243) It was during this period that the political strategy of the labor movement would solidify.[[231]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESternhell1999219-244) The founding of the Mapai party unified the labor movement, making it the dominant force. The party saw economic control as essential to facilitating Zionist settlement and achieving political power: "the economic question is not one of class; it is a national question".[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESternhell1999-245) For Ben-Gurion, the transformation from "working class to nation" was intertwined with his rejection of diaspora life, as he would declare: the "weak, unproductive, parasitical Jewish masses" must be converted "to productive labor" in service of the nation.[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDieckhoff200389–95-246)

### Zionist policies and the 1936–1939 Arab Revolt

For the Zionist movement, economic development and policies were a mechanism by which political aims could be achieved.[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESternhell1999-245) A new economic sector exclusively for Jews, controlled by the Labor Zionist movement, was established with support from the Jewish National Fund and the agricultural workers' Histadrut.
