Despite the universalist ideals of Zionist pioneering, this new Jewish economic sector was fundamentally based on exclusionary practices.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShafir1996-170) The goal of achieving "100 percent of Hebrew labour" was the primary driver of the territorial, economic and social separation between Jews and Arabs.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlapan1979The_Policy_of_Economic_and_Social_Separation-115)

The Zionist economic platform was partially based on the assumption (eventually demonstrated incorrect[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlapan197919-247)) that economic benefits to the Arabs of Palestine would pacify opposition to the movement. For the Zionist leadership, the economic status and development of the Arabs of Palestine should be compared with Arabs of other countries, rather than with the Jews of Palestine. Accordingly, disproportionate gains in Jewish development were acceptable as long as the status of the Arab sector did not worsen. While British support for Zionist aspirations in Palestine established the parameters within which the Arab economy could develop, Zionist policies reinforced these limitations. Most notable are the exclusion of Arab labor from Jewish enterprise and the expulsion of Arab peasants from Jewish-owned land. Both of these had limited impact in scope but reinforced the structural limitations put in place by British policies.[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoy2016The_Economic_Transformation_of_Palestine:_Key_British_and_Zionst_Policies-248)

With the rise to power of the Nazis in 1933, the Jewish community was increasingly persecuted and driven out. The discriminatory immigration laws of the US, UK and other countries preferable to German Jews, led to in 1935 alone more than 60,000 Jews arriving in Palestine (more than the total number of Jews in Palestine as of the establishment of the Balfour declaration in 1917). Ben-Gurion would subsequently declare that immigration at this rate would allow for the maximalist Zionist goal of a Jewish state in all of Palestine.[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPappé2004-249)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] The Arab community openly pressured the mandatory government to restrict Jewish immigration and land purchases.[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoy201633-250)

Sporadic attacks in the countryside (described by Zionists and the British as "banditry") reflected widespread anger over the Zionist land purchases that displaced local peasants. Meanwhile, in urban areas, protests against British rule and the increasing influence of the Zionist movement intensified and became more militant in the early 1930s.[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhalidi202044-251)

The outbreak of violence in the course of the 1936 Arab Revolt was a turning point in Jewish-Arab relations, unifying previously divided factions within the Zionist movement and leading them to view the use of force as a necessary means of defense and deterrence.[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGorny1987243,_251-252)

During the revolt, the [Irgun Zvai Leumi](/wiki/Irgun_Zvai_Leumi "Irgun Zvai Leumi") engaged in the use of terror attacks against the Arabs of Palestine. Similarly, for the labor Zionist [Palmah](/wiki/Palmah "Palmah"), the lines delineating what was acceptable and unacceptable while dealing with Arab villagers were "vague and intentionally blurred". These ambiguous limits practically did not differ from those of the self-described "terrorist" group, Irgun. According to Anita Shapira, beginning in this period, Labor Zionism's use of violence against Palestinians for political means was essentially the same as that of radical conservative Zionist groups.[[240]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShapira1992247,_249,_251–252,_350,_365-253)

### The Peel Commission partition proposal

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/PeelMap.png/250px-PeelMap.png)](/wiki/File:PeelMap.png)

Provisional frontiers of the Palestine partition from the Peel Commission

In response to the revolt, the British appointed in 1937 a [commission of inquiry](/wiki/Peel_Commission "Peel Commission") that eventually recommended the partition of the land. The proposal included creating a small Jewish state occupying 17 percent of Mandatory Palestine's territory,[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhalidi200645-254) while Jerusalem and a corridor to the sea would remain under British control, and the remaining 75 percent of the territory would form a Palestinian state connected to Transjordan under King Abdullah's rule.[[242]](#cite_note-255)[[243]](#cite_note-256) At this point, Jews owned 5.6% of the land in Palestine; the land allocated to the Jewish state would contain 40 percent of the country's fertile land.[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoy201633-250)
The commission also proposed a [population transfer](/wiki/Population_transfer "Population transfer") of the Palestinian Arabs from the areas designated for the Jewish state, based on the precedent of the [1923 Greek-Turkish population exchange](/wiki/1923_Greek-Turkish_population_exchange "1923 Greek-Turkish population exchange").[[244]](#cite_note-257)[[245]](#cite_note-258) For Ben-Gurion, the transfer proposal was the most appealing recommendation put forward by the commission.[[246]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBen-Ami200725-259) Indeed, this sentiment was deeply ingrained to the extent that Ben Gurion's acceptance of partition was contingent upon the removal of the Palestinian population.[[247]](#cite_note-260)

The Zionist leadership viewed the mass transfer of the Arabs as morally permissible, but were unsure of its political effectiveness.[[248]](#cite_note-261) Much of the Zionist leadership spoke in strong support of the transfer plan, asserting that there is nothing immoral about it.[[n]](#cite_note-263) Within the Zionist movement, two perspectives developed with respect to the partition proposal; the first was a complete rejection of partition, the second was acceptance of the idea of partition on the basis that it would eventually allow for expansion to all territories within "the boundaries of Zionist aspirations."[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChomsky1982-264) It was the right wing of the Zionist movement that put forward the main arguments against transfer, with Jabotinsky strongly objecting it on moral grounds,[[251]](#cite_note-265) and others mainly focusing on its impracticality.[[252]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlapan1979264-266) However, in his last book "The Jewish War Front" published in 1940, after the outbreak of WWII, Jabotinsky no longer ruled out the possibility of voluntary population transfer, though he still didn't view it as a necessary solution.[[253]](#cite_note-267)[[254]](#cite_note-268)[[255]](#cite_note-269)[[256]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESegev2001406–407-270) Some leaders, such as Ruppin, [Motzkin](/wiki/Leo_Motzkin "Leo Motzkin"), and writers such as [Israel Zangwill](/wiki/Israel_Zangwill "Israel Zangwill"), also referred to transfer as a "voluntary" action that would include some form of compensation.[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris2009349–360-271) However, "Palestine's Arabs did not wish to evacuate the land of their ancestors... The matter raised ethical questions that troubled the Yishuv".[[258]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris2001140-272) The revolt was inflamed by the partition proposal and continued until 1939 when it was forcefully suppressed by the British.[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhalidi2020Chapter_1-232)

Later, Vladimir Jabotinsky, the right-wing Zionist leader, drew inspiration from the Nazi demographic policies that resulted in the expulsion of 1.5 million Poles and Jews, in whose place Germans resettled.[[259]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFinkelstein2016-273) In Jabotinsky's assessment:

> The world has become accustomed to the idea of mass migrations and has almost become fond of them. Hitler—as odious as he is to us—has given this idea a good name in the world.[[259]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFinkelstein2016-273)

By the time of the [1936 Arab revolt](/wiki/1936_Arab_revolt "1936 Arab revolt"), almost all groups within the Zionist movement wanted a Jewish state in Palestine, "whether they declared their intent or preferred to camouflage it, whether or not they perceived it as a political instrument, whether they saw sovereign independence as the prime aim, or accorded priority to the task of social construction".[[260]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGorny1987243-245-274) The main debates within the movement at this time were concerning partition of Palestine and the nature of the relationship with the British. The intensity of the revolt, Britain's ambiguous support for the movement and the increasing threat against European Jewry during this period motivated the Zionist leadership to prioritize immediate considerations. The movement ultimately favored the notion of partition, primarily out of practical considerations and partially out of a belief that establishing a Jewish state over all of Palestine would remain an option.[[261]](#cite_note-275) At the 1937 Zionist congress, the Zionist leadership adopted the stance that the land allocated to the Jewish state by the partition plan was inadequate—effectively rejecting the partition plan that faded away in the face of both Arab and Zionist opposition.[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECleveland2010Communal_Conflict_and_the_British_Response-276)

In response to Ben-Gurion's 1938 quote that "politically we are the aggressors and they [the Palestinians] defend themselves", Israeli historian [Benny Morris](/wiki/Benny_Morris "Benny Morris") says, "Ben-Gurion, of course, was right. Zionism was a colonizing and expansionist ideology and movement", and that "Zionist ideology and practice were necessarily and elementally expansionist." Morris describes the Zionist goal of establishing a Jewish state in Palestine as necessarily displacing and dispossessing the Arab population.[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris2001Conclusions-277)

### Nazism, World War II and the Holocaust

In 1939, a [British White Paper](/wiki/British_White_Paper "British White Paper") recommended limiting Jewish immigration and land purchase with the objective of maintaining the status quo while the threat of war loomed in Europe.[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris1999162-278)[[265]](#cite_note-279) The immigration was to be limited to no more than 75,000 people over the next five years. With Nazi expansionism in Europe, the limits on immigration prompted further militarization, land takeover and illegal immigration efforts by the Zionist movement. World War II broke out as the Zionists were developing their campaign against the White Paper—unable to accept the White Paper or to side against the British, the Zionist movement would ultimately support the British war effort while working to upend the White Paper.[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECleveland2010Communal_Conflict_and_the_British_Response-276)[[o]](#cite_note-280) From the start of the second world war, the Zionists pressured the British to organize and train a Jewish "army", culminating in the establishment of a Jewish Brigade and accompanying blue and white flag.[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris1999167-281)[[267]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGorny1987277-282) The development of this force would further train and enable the already substantial Zionist military capacity.[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhalidi2020Chapter_1-232)[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris1999167-281)[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPappé2004-249)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] The Haganah was allowed by the British to openly acquire weapons and worked with the British to prepare for a possible Axis invasion.[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECleveland2010World_War_II_and_the_Birth_of_the_State_of_Israel-283)

Despite the White Paper, Zionist immigration and settlement efforts continued during the war period. While immigration had previously been selective, once the details of the Holocaust reached Palestine in 1942, selectivity was abandoned. The official Zionist movement's war effort focused on the survival and development of the Yishuv; Pappe argues that scarce Zionist energy was deployed in support of European Jews.[[269]](#cite_note-284) Many of those fleeing Nazi terror in Europe preferred to leave for the United States, however, strict American immigration policies and Zionist efforts led to 10% of the 3 million Jews leaving Europe to settle in Palestine.[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPappé2004Palestine_in_World_War_II-285)

In the [Biltmore Program](/wiki/Biltmore_Program "Biltmore Program") of 1942, the Zionist movement would openly declare for the first time its goal of establishing a Jewish state in all of Palestine.[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris1999169-286)[[272]](#cite_note-287) At this point, the United States, with its growing economy and unprecedented military force, became a focal point of Zionist political activity that engaged with the American electorate and politicians. American [President Truman](/wiki/President_Truman "President Truman") supported the Biltmore program for the duration of his time in office, largely motivated by humanitarian concerns and the growing influence of the Zionist lobby.[[273]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECleveland2010-288)

During World War II, as the horrors of [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust") became known, the Zionist leadership formulated the [One Million Plan](/wiki/One_Million_Plan "One Million Plan"), a reduction from Ben-Gurion's previous target of two million immigrants. Following the end of the war, many [stateless refugees](/wiki/Sh%27erit_ha-Pletah "Sh'erit ha-Pletah"), mainly [Holocaust survivors](/wiki/Holocaust_survivors "Holocaust survivors"), began [migrating to Palestine](/wiki/Aliyah_Bet "Aliyah Bet") in small boats in defiance of British rules. The Holocaust united much of the rest of world Jewry behind the Zionist project.[[275]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnson199821–28-290) The British either [imprisoned these Jews in Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus_internment_camps "Cyprus internment camps") or [sent them](/wiki/SS_Exodus "SS Exodus") to the British-controlled [Allied Occupation Zones in Germany](/wiki/Allied_Occupation_Zones_in_Germany "Allied Occupation Zones in Germany"). The British, having faced Arab revolts, were now facing opposition by [Zionist groups in Palestine](/wiki/British%E2%80%93Zionist_conflict "British–Zionist conflict") for subsequent restrictions on Jewish immigration. In January 1946 the Anglo-American Committee of Inquiry, a joint [British and American](/wiki/United_Kingdom%E2%80%93United_States_relations "United Kingdom–United States relations") committee, was tasked to examine political, economic and social conditions in Mandatory Palestine and the well-being of the peoples now living there; to consult representatives of Arabs and Jews, and to make other recommendations 'as necessary' for an interim handling of these problems as well as for their eventual solution.[[276]](#cite_note-291) Following the failure of the [1946–47 London Conference on Palestine](/wiki/London_Conference_of_1946%E2%80%9347 "London Conference of 1946–47"), at which the United States refused to support the British, leading to both the [Morrison–Grady Plan](/wiki/Morrison%E2%80%93Grady_Plan "Morrison–Grady Plan") and the [Bevin Plan](/wiki/Bevin_Plan "Bevin Plan") being rejected by all parties, the British decided to refer the question to the UN on February 14, 1947.[[277]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERavndal2010-292)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[p]](#cite_note-293)

### End of the Mandate and expulsion of the Palestinians

Towards the end of the war, the Zionist leadership was motivated more than ever to establish a Jewish state. Since the British were no longer sponsoring its development, many Zionists considered it would be necessary to [establish the state by force](/wiki/Zionist_political_violence "Zionist political violence") by upending the British position in Palestine. In this the [IRA's tactics](/wiki/Irish_Republican_Army "Irish Republican Army") against Britain in the [Irish War of Independence](/wiki/Irish_War_of_Independence "Irish War of Independence") served as a both a model and source of inspiration.[[q]](#cite_note-295) The [Irgun](/wiki/Irgun "Irgun"), the military arm of the revisionist Zionists, and [Lehi](/wiki/Lehi_(militant_group) "Lehi (militant group)"), who at one point sought an alliance with the Nazis,[[279]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris1999174-296) would lead a series of terrorist attacks against the British starting in 1944. This included the [King David Hotel bombing](/wiki/King_David_Hotel_bombing "King David Hotel bombing"), British immigration and tax offices and police stations. It was only by the war's end that the Haganah joined in the sabotage against the British. The combined impact of US opinion and the attacks on British presence eventually led the British to refer the situation to the United Nations in 1947.[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECleveland2010World_War_II_and_the_Birth_of_the_State_of_Israel-283) In response to a United Kingdom government request that the General Assembly "make recommendations under article 10 of the Charter, concerning the future government of Palestine", the [United Nations Special Committee on Palestine](/wiki/United_Nations_Special_Committee_on_Palestine "United Nations Special Committee on Palestine") (UNSCOP) was created on May 15, 1947.[[280]](#cite_note-297)

The urgency of the condition of the Jewish refugees in Europe motivated the committee to unanimously vote in favor of terminating the British mandate in Palestine. The disagreement came with regards to whether Palestine should be partitioned or if it should constitute a federal state. American lobbying efforts, pressuring UN delegates with the threat of withdrawal of US aid, eventually secured the General Assembly votes in favor of the partition of Palestine into separate Jewish and Arab states, which was passed November 29, 1947.[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECleveland2010World_War_II_and_the_Birth_of_the_State_of_Israel-283)

Outbursts of violence slowly grew into a wider civil war between the Arabs and Zionist militias.[[281]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPappé2004118-119-298) By mid-December, the Haganah had shifted to a more "aggressive defense",[[282]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris1999148-299) abandoning notions of restraint it had espoused from 1936 to 1939. The Haganah reprisal raids were often disproportionate to the initial Arab offenses,[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris1999196–197-300) which led to the spread of violence to previously unaffected areas. The Zionist militias, employed terror attacks against Arab civilian and militia centers and many Palestinians were evicted from their houses. In response, Arabs planted bombs in Jewish civilian areas, particularly in Jerusalem.[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris1999196–197-300)[[284]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPappé2004127-301)

The first expulsion of Palestinians began 12 days after the adoption of the UN resolution, and the first Palestinian village was eliminated[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] a month later, in retaliation for Palestinian attacks on convoys and Jewish settlements.[[285]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPappé2004118–119-302) In March 1948, Zionist forces began implementing Plan D, an ethnic cleansing operation that involved the expulsion of civilians and the destruction of Arab towns and villages in pursuit of eliminating Palestinians seen as potentially hostile, resulting in the loss of Palestine to much of its indigenous population.[[286]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPappé2004120-303)[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShlaim2001Introduction-168)[[287]](#cite_note-304) According to [Benny Morris](/wiki/Benny_Morris "Benny Morris") Zionist forces committed 24 massacres of Palestinians in the ensuing war,[[288]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris2008404–406-305)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] in part as a form of psychological warfare,[[289]](#cite_note-306) the most notorious of which is the [Deir Yassin massacre](/wiki/Deir_Yassin_massacre "Deir Yassin massacre"). The [United Nations Conciliation Commission for Palestine](/wiki/United_Nations_Conciliation_Commission_for_Palestine "United Nations Conciliation Commission for Palestine") estimated that between 1948 and 1949, 710,000 Palestinians were driven out of the country and another 40,000 were [internally displaced](/wiki/Present_absentees "Present absentees"), primarily as a result of these expulsions and massacres.[[14]](#cite_note-unccp-15)[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPappé2004-249)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

The British left Palestine (having done little to maintain order) on May 14 as planned. The British did not facilitate a formal transfer of power;[[273]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECleveland2010-288) a fully functioning Jewish quasi-state had already been operating under the British for the past several decades.[[290]](#cite_note-307) The same day, Ben-Gurion declared the establishment of the state of Israel.[[291]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECleveland2010Terror_and_Intercommunal_War-308) The [Declaration of Independence of Israel](/wiki/Declaration_of_Independence_of_Israel "Declaration of Independence of Israel") described a democracy with equality of social and political rights for all citizens, and extended a peace offering to neighboring states and their Arab citizens.[[292]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShapira2012180-309) Masalha notes that the declaration states equality on the basis of citizenship but not nationality.[[r]](#cite_note-310)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

The ensuing war led to the State of Israel establishing control over 78% of Mandatory Palestine, instead of the 55% outlined in the UN partition plan, and resulted in the destruction of much of Palestinian society and the Arab landscape.[[293]](#cite_note-311) This war, led by the Zionist Yishuv was framed by its leaders in biblical and messianic terms as a 'miraculous clearing of the land,' akin to the biblical War of Joshua. Masalha writes that it is not clear who the Yishuv was declaring independence from, as it was neither from the British colonial rule, which facilitated Jewish settlement against Palestinian wishes, nor from the land's indigenous inhabitants, who had long cultivated and owned it.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasalha2012Chapter_1-81) After the [1949 Armistice Agreements](/wiki/1949_Armistice_Agreements "1949 Armistice Agreements"), [a series of laws](/wiki/Land_and_Property_laws_in_Israel#The_'Absentees_Property_Law' "Land and Property laws in Israel") passed by the first Israeli government prevented [displaced Palestinians](/wiki/Palestinian_refugees "Palestinian refugees") from claiming private property or returning on the state's territories. They and many of their descendants remain [refugees](/wiki/Palestinian_refugees "Palestinian refugees") supported by [UNRWA](/wiki/UNRWA "UNRWA").[[294]](#cite_note-312)[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[295]](#cite_note-313)[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[296]](#cite_note-314)

#### Hebraization of names

Main article: [Hebraization of Palestinian place names](/wiki/Hebraization_of_Palestinian_place_names "Hebraization of Palestinian place names")

As part of the effort to consolidate its new ownership over the land it had taken over in the 1948 war, the Israeli state worked towards "erasing all traces of its former owners".[[297]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShapira2014248-315) The project of "Hebraization" of the map, for which the JNF Naming Committee was established,[[298]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasalha2012The_Zionist_Superimposing_of_Hebrew_Toponymy-316) aimed to replace what remained of the Arab towns and villages with newly named Israeli settlements. These names were often based on the Arab names but with a "Hebrew pronunciation" or based on old Hebrew biblical names.[[297]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShapira2014248-315) This effort also sought to demonstrate continuous Jewish ownership over the land to ancient times.[[297]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShapira2014248-315)

Prior to 1948, the Zionist movement had limited authority over the use of place names in Palestine. After 1948, the name "Palestine" was removed from Zionist organizations; for example, the [Jewish Agency for Palestine](/wiki/Jewish_Agency_for_Palestine "Jewish Agency for Palestine"), which played a critical role in the founding of the Israeli state in 1948 was renamed to the "Jewish Agency for Israel".[[299]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasalha2012-317)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

### Post-World War II

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/1948_Arab_Israeli_War_-_May_15-June_10.svg/250px-1948_Arab_Israeli_War_-_May_15-June_10.svg.png)](/wiki/File:1948_Arab_Israeli_War_-_May_15-June_10.svg)

Arab offensive at the beginning of the [1948 Arab–Israeli war](/wiki/1948_Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_war "1948 Arab–Israeli war")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Declaration_of_State_of_Israel_1948_2.jpg/250px-Declaration_of_State_of_Israel_1948_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Declaration_of_State_of_Israel_1948_2.jpg)

[David Ben-Gurion](/wiki/David_Ben-Gurion "David Ben-Gurion") proclaiming Israel's establishment beneath a large portrait of Theodor Herzl

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Op_Magic_Carpet_%28Yemenites%29.jpg/250px-Op_Magic_Carpet_%28Yemenites%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Op_Magic_Carpet_(Yemenites).jpg)

Yemenite Jews on their way to Israel during [Operation Magic Carpet](/wiki/Operation_Magic_Carpet_(Yemen) "Operation Magic Carpet (Yemen)")

Since the creation of the State of Israel, the World Zionist Organization has functioned mainly as an organization dedicated to assisting and encouraging Jews to migrate to Israel. It has provided political support for Israel in other countries but plays little role in internal Israeli politics. The movement's major success since 1948 was in providing logistical support for Jewish migrants and refugees and, most importantly, in assisting [Soviet Jews](/wiki/Soviet_Jews "Soviet Jews") in their struggle with the authorities over the right to leave the USSR and to practice their religion in freedom, and the [exodus of 850,000 Jews](/wiki/Jewish_exodus_from_Arab_lands "Jewish exodus from Arab lands") from the Arab world, mostly to Israel. In 1944–45, Ben-Gurion described the [One Million Plan](/wiki/One_Million_Plan "One Million Plan") to foreign officials as being the "primary goal and top priority of the Zionist movement."[[300]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHacohen1991262_#2-318) The immigration restrictions of the British White Paper of 1939 meant that such a plan could not be put into large scale effect until the Israeli Declaration of Independence in May 1948. The new country's immigration policy had some opposition within the new Israeli government, such as those who argued that there was "no justification for organizing large-scale emigration among Jews whose lives were not in danger, particularly when the desire and motivation were not their own"[[301]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHakohen200346-319) as well as those who argued that the absorption process caused "undue hardship".[[302]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHakohen2003246–247-320) However, the force of Ben-Gurion's influence and insistence ensured that his immigration policy was carried out.[[303]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHakohen200347-321)[[304]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHakohen2003247-322)

### Religious Zionism and the Six-Day War

Main article: [Neo-Zionism](/wiki/Neo-Zionism "Neo-Zionism")

Prior to the 1967 [Six-Day War](/wiki/Six-Day_War "Six-Day War"), [religious Zionism](/wiki/Religious_Zionism "Religious Zionism") mostly described support for political Zionism among Orthodox Jews.[[305]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERavitzky199632–34-323) However, the war and the Israeli conquest of the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank") invigorated and popularized a religious Zionist ideology associated with the Rabbi [Abraham Isaac Kook](/wiki/Abraham_Isaac_Kook "Abraham Isaac Kook") and the [Mercaz HaRav](/wiki/Mercaz_HaRav "Mercaz HaRav") yeshiva, believing Zionism part of a process leading to redemption and the [messianic age](/wiki/Messianic_age "Messianic age").[[306]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERavitzky1996102–109,_122–131-324)[[307]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShlaim2001Chapter_14-325) This ideology considered secular Zionism and secular state policies to be holy and part of God's divine plan.[[308]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoldberg2009-326)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

According to followers of the Mercaz HaRav ideology, the Six Day War was a demonstration of the work of the Divine Hand and the "beginning of redemption".[[309]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERavitzky1996Chapter_3-327) Proponents of this ideology venerate the land as sacred, and consider its sanctity a core principle of religious Zionism. Religious Zionists view the settlement of the West Bank as a commandment of God, necessary for the redemption of the Jewish people.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBen-Ami2007-59) Rabbi [Zvi Yehuda Kook](/wiki/Zvi_Yehuda_Kook "Zvi Yehuda Kook"), a major religious Zionist leader and thinker, declared in 1967 following the Six Day War in the presence of Israeli leadership including the president, ministers, members of the [Knesset](/wiki/Knesset "Knesset"), judges, chief rabbis and senior civil servants:

> I tell you explicitly... that there is a prohibition in the Torah against giving up even an inch of our liberated land. There are no conquests here and we are not occupying foreign land; we are returning to our home, to the inheritance of our forefathers. There is no Arab land here, only the inheritance of our God—the more the world gets used to this thought the better it will be for it and for all of us.[[310]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasalha2014-328)

In the 1970s, religious Zionists, such as [Shlomo Aviner](/wiki/Shlomo_Aviner "Shlomo Aviner") and [Hanan Porat](/wiki/Hanan_Porat "Hanan Porat"), campaigned against Israeli withdrawal from the West Bank and [Sinai Peninsula](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula").[[309]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERavitzky1996Chapter_3-327) Religious Zionist ideology motivated the [assassination of Yitzhak Rabin](/wiki/Assassination_of_Yitzhak_Rabin "Assassination of Yitzhak Rabin") in 1995, who ceded some territory to the [PLO](/wiki/PLO "PLO") as a part of the [Oslo Accords](/wiki/Oslo_Accords "Oslo Accords"), and several religious Zionist rabbis reacted to the assassination with approval.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShlaim2001-212)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

## Types of Zionism

Main article: [Types of Zionism](/wiki/Types_of_Zionism "Types of Zionism")

See also: [Post-Zionism](/wiki/Post-Zionism "Post-Zionism")

From the turn of the century until the Arab revolt of 1936, there was room for political flexibility within the Zionist movement, many scholars argue that different currents of Zionism have had a shared core framework.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGorny1987-205)[[311]](#cite_note-329) Most mainstream histories of the movement delineate a few key strains, many following a taxonomy introduced during the period starting in the late 19th century and continuing into the 1930s: political, practical, socialist, cultural and revisionist.[[312]](#cite_note-330)[[313]](#cite_note-Shoham-2013-331) Some scholars emphasise the heterogeneity of these strains of Zionism.[[314]](#cite_note-332)[[315]](#cite_note-333)[[316]](#cite_note-334)

### Early Zionism

Early Zionism consisted of two dominant types, [Political Zionism](/wiki/Political_Zionism "Political Zionism") and [Practical Zionism](/wiki/Practical_Zionism "Practical Zionism"), which faded after [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and the [Balfour Declaration](/wiki/Balfour_Declaration "Balfour Declaration").[[317]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPenslar202336–39-335)

Political Zionism was led by [Theodor Herzl](/wiki/Theodor_Herzl "Theodor Herzl") and [Max Nordau](/wiki/Max_Nordau "Max Nordau"). This approach was espoused at the [Zionist Organization](/wiki/Zionist_Organization "Zionist Organization")'s [First Zionist Congress](/wiki/First_Zionist_Congress "First Zionist Congress"). It focused on a Jewish home as a solution to the "[Jewish question](/wiki/Jewish_question "Jewish question")" and antisemitism in Europe, centered on gaining Jewish sovereignty (probably within the Ottoman or later British or French empire), and was opposed to mass migration until after sovereignty was granted. It initially considered locations other than Palestine (e.g., in Africa) and did not foresee migration by many Western Jews to the new homeland.[[318]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBerent2019-336)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[313]](#cite_note-Shoham-2013-331)

Known in Hebrew as *Tzionut Ma'asit*, Practical Zionism was led by [Moshe Leib Lilienblum](/wiki/Moshe_Leib_Lilienblum "Moshe Leib Lilienblum") and [Leon Pinsker](/wiki/Leon_Pinsker "Leon Pinsker") and molded by the [Hovevei Zion](/wiki/Hovevei_Zion "Hovevei Zion") organization.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] It became dominant after Herzl's death, and differed from Political Zionism in not seeing Zionism as justified primarily by the Jewish Question but rather as an end in itself; it "aspired to the establishment of an elite utopian community in Palestine" through [Aliyah](/wiki/Aliyah "Aliyah").[[318]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBerent2019-336)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] It also differed from Political Zionism in "distrust[ing] grand political actions" and preferring "an evolutionary incremental process toward the establishment of the national home".[[318]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBerent2019-336)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[313]](#cite_note-Shoham-2013-331)

### Labor Zionism

Main article: [Labor Zionism](/wiki/Labor_Zionism "Labor Zionism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Ber_Borochov.jpg/120px-Ber_Borochov.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ber_Borochov.jpg)

[Dov Ber Borochov](/wiki/Ber_Borochov "Ber Borochov"), one of the leaders of Labor Zionism

Led by socialists [Nachman Syrkin](/wiki/Nachman_Syrkin "Nachman Syrkin"), [Haim Arlosoroff](/wiki/Haim_Arlosoroff "Haim Arlosoroff"), [Berl Katznelson](/wiki/Berl_Katznelson "Berl Katznelson"), and Marxist [Ber Borochov](/wiki/Ber_Borochov "Ber Borochov"),[[319]](#cite_note-337)[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShafir1996-170) Labor or Socialist Zionism was a form of Zionism that combined messianic tendencies and [socialist](/wiki/Socialist "Socialist") or [social democratic](/wiki/Social_democratic "Social democratic") politics.[[320]](#cite_note-Perlmutter-1969-338)[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShafir1996-170) The labor Zionists promoted immigration and settlement, establishing "facts on the ground" as the main path towards state-building.[[321]](#cite_note-339)

Labor Zionism became a mass movement with the founding of [Poale Zion](/wiki/Poale_Zion "Poale Zion") ("Workers of Zion") groups in Eastern and Western Europe and North America in the 1900s.[[322]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeßler2019-340) Poale Zion split between Left and Right after 1917. In 1919, the Right Poale Zion in Palestine disbanded to form the nationalist socialist [Ahdut HaAvoda](/wiki/Ahdut_HaAvoda "Ahdut HaAvoda"), led by [David Ben Gurion](/wiki/David_Ben_Gurion "David Ben Gurion");[[323]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETeveth198566–70-341)[[324]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGetzoff2019-342)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[325]](#cite_note-343) in 1930, it merged with [Hapoel Hatzair](/wiki/Hapoel_Hatzair "Hapoel Hatzair"), founded by [A. D. Gordon](/wiki/A._D._Gordon "A. D. Gordon"), to form Mapai.[[326]](#cite_note-344)[[324]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGetzoff2019-342)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Labor Zionism, represented by Mapai, became the dominant force in the political and economic life of the [Yishuv](/wiki/Yishuv "Yishuv") during the [British Mandate of Palestine](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine"). It was the dominant ideology of the political establishment in Israel until the [1977 election](/wiki/1977_Israeli_legislative_election "1977 Israeli legislative election"), when the [Israeli Labor Party](/wiki/Israeli_Labor_Party "Israeli Labor Party") was defeated.[[320]](#cite_note-Perlmutter-1969-338) During the early twentieth century, the left wing of this tradition was represented by [Hashomer Hatzair](/wiki/Hashomer_Hatzair "Hashomer Hatzair"), followed by [Mapam](/wiki/Mapam "Mapam") in the late twentieth century, and [Meretz](/wiki/Meretz "Meretz") until 2022.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Mishmar_HaEmek.JPG/250px-Mishmar_HaEmek.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mishmar_HaEmek.JPG)

Kibbutznikiyot (female Kibbutz members) in [Mishmar HaEmek](/wiki/Mishmar_HaEmek "Mishmar HaEmek"), during the [1948 Arab–Israeli War](/wiki/1948_Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_War "1948 Arab–Israeli War"). The [Kibbutz](/wiki/Kibbutz "Kibbutz") is the historical heartland of Labor Zionism.

In Labor Zionist thought, a revolution of the Jewish soul and society was believed necessary and achievable in part by Jews moving to [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") and becoming farmers, workers, and soldiers in a country of their own. Labor Zionists established rural communes in Israel called "[kibbutzim](/wiki/Kibbutz "Kibbutz")",[[327]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENear1986-345)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] a form of [cooperative agriculture](/wiki/Collective_farming "Collective farming") in which the [Jewish National Fund](/wiki/Jewish_National_Fund "Jewish National Fund") hired Jewish workers under trained supervision. The kibbutzim were a symbol of the [Second Aliyah](/wiki/Second_Aliyah "Second Aliyah") in that they put great emphasis on communalism and egalitarianism, representing [Utopian socialism](/wiki/Utopian_socialism "Utopian socialism") to a certain extent. Furthermore, they stressed self-sufficiency, which became an essential aspect of Labor Zionism.[[328]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESternhellMaisel1998-346)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[329]](#cite_note-347)

### Synthetic and General Zionism

Main article: [General Zionists](/wiki/General_Zionists "General Zionists")

Synthetic Zionism, led by [Chaim Weizmann](/wiki/Chaim_Weizmann "Chaim Weizmann"), [Leo Motzkin](/wiki/Leo_Motzkin "Leo Motzkin") and [Nahum Sokolow](/wiki/Nahum_Sokolow "Nahum Sokolow"), was an approach that advocated a combination of practical and political Zionism[[330]](#cite_note-348) and distanced themselves from the growing Labour, Religious and Revisionist Zionist groups as the movement became polarised between those.[[331]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoldstein2018-349)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[332]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDivine1993316–318-350) General Zionists identified with the liberal European middle class to which many Zionist leaders such as Herzl and [Chaim Weizmann](/wiki/Chaim_Weizmann "Chaim Weizmann") aspired. As head of the World Zionist Organization, Weizmann's policies had a sustained impact on the Zionist movement, with Abba Eban describing him as a dominant figure in Jewish life during the interwar period.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Revisionist Zionism

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Zeev_Jabotinsky_portrait.png/250px-Zeev_Jabotinsky_portrait.png)](/wiki/File:Zeev_Jabotinsky_portrait.png)

[Ze'ev Jabotinsky](/wiki/Ze%27ev_Jabotinsky "Ze'ev Jabotinsky"), founder of Revisionist Zionism

Main article: [Revisionist Zionism](/wiki/Revisionist_Zionism "Revisionist Zionism")

Revisionist Zionism, developed by [Ze'ev Jabotinsky](/wiki/Ze%27ev_Jabotinsky "Ze'ev Jabotinsky") in the 1920s, was a right-wing strain of Zionism. It initially believed that a Jewish state must expand to both sides of the [Jordan River](/wiki/Jordan_River "Jordan River"), i.e. taking [Transjordan](/wiki/Transjordan_(region) "Transjordan (region)") in addition to all of Palestine.[[333]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZouplna2008-351)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[334]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShlaim1996-352)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] It broke with the WZO in 1935 because of the latter's refusal to declare the establishment of a Jewish state as its immediate aim,[[334]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShlaim1996-352)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] and had its own paramilitary organisation, [Irgun](/wiki/Irgun "Irgun"), from 1931 to 1948.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Followers of Revisionist Zionism developed the [Likud](/wiki/Likud "Likud") Party in Israel, which has dominated most governments since 1977. It advocates Israel's maintaining control of the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank"), including [East Jerusalem](/wiki/East_Jerusalem "East Jerusalem"), and takes a hard-line approach in the Arab–Israeli conflict.[[335]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVauseRazMedding2005-353)
